coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) are flexible and promising family of nanomaterials that have gained attention due to the rapidly developing field of nanotechnology. The biomass derived carbon dots (BCDs) are sustainable materials obeying green chemistry principles and are derived from abundant and renewable natural resources. Due to modern industrialization, waste generated and disposed to the environment causes pollution which in turn has negative effects on human and animal life. It is crucial to build sensor systems that can detect contaminants and helps in controlling water pollution. In this context, fluorescent BCDs are appropriate sensor materials due to their outstanding selectivity and sensitivity for recognizing analytes, providing an efficient and reliable method for monitoring the environment as compared to other techniques. This work presents the recent advancement of eco-friendly BCDs from natural sources with remarkable fluorescence properties and its application in detection of different analytes.

head.ce:title: Recent development of biomass derived carbon dots in fluorescent sensing applications

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) are flexible and promising family of nanomaterials that have gained attention due to the rapidly developing field of nanotechnology. The biomass derived carbon dots (BCDs) are sustainable materials obeying green chemistry principles and are derived from abundant and renewable natural resources. Due to modern industrialization, waste generated and disposed to the environment causes pollution which in turn has negative effects on human and animal life. It is crucial to build sensor systems that can detect contaminants and helps in controlling water pollution. In this context, fluorescent BCDs are appropriate sensor materials due to their outstanding selectivity and sensitivity for recognizing analytes, providing an efficient and reliable method for monitoring the environment as compared to other techniques. This work presents the recent advancement of eco-friendly BCDs from natural sources with remarkable fluorescence properties and its application in detection of different analytes.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nature is an abundance of fascinating starting materials, including numerous inexpensive and renewable resources that have attracted the most attention due to their non-hazardous and eco-friendly nature. These materials can be utilized to make biomass derived carbon dots (BCDs). Utilizing a variety of biomass wastes and derivatives for producing BCDs is a green synthesis method with a lot of commercialization potential because it provides abundant, sustainable, and economically feasible materials. The process yields high-quality BCDs with regulated surface structure, size and high fluorescence making it trustworthy and reusable (Khan et al., 2024).

The literature mainly offers two ways for synthesizing BCDs namely the top-down approach and the bottom-up approach. The top-down strategy deals with the breaking down of larger carbon structure into nanoscale particles, whereas the bottom-up approach synthesizes BCDs from smaller carbon units (Sharma and Das, 2019; Meng et al., 2019). The top-down approaches require a harsh reaction environment, apparatus, expensive materials, and a long reaction time. Whereas, the bottom-up approaches are the most extensively utilized method for BCDs production due to their mass production, low cost, and ecological sustainability (Modi et al., 2023).

BCDs differ from chemically generated CDs because they have lower toxicity, higher water dispersibility, greater biocompatibility, good photostability, bright fluorescence and are easier to modify. Moreover, BCDs are often made up of sp2 and sp3 hybrid carbon nuclei with a high concentration of active functional groups such as -OH, -COOH, -NH2, and so on; resulting in great water solubility (Vinoth Kumar et al., 2021; Chai et al., 2022). The advantages of BCDs are presented in Fig. 1 . In recent years, variety of methods has been employed to produce BCDs from natural biomasses. Among several techniques, the hydrothermal method is the most often used technique for BCD synthesis. Many research groups have reported the production of BCDs using plant biomasses like orange juice (Kasirajan et al., 2024), cassava pulp (Watcharamongkal et al., 2024), camphor tree waste leaves (Y.S. Li et al., 2024), walnut green skin (Xing et al., 2024), Boerhavia diffusa leaves (Kaur et al., 2025), water amaranth leaves (Keerthana et al., 2023), Aloe carazo leaf (J. Wang et al., 2024a), bamboo stem (Adaikalapandi et al., 2024), Ginkgo biloba L. leaves (Fei and Sun, 2024), Okra (Mei et al., 2024), broccoli leaves (Man et al., 2023), Plumeria obtusa leaves (Sharma and Chowdhury, 2024), mopan persimmons (Ma et al., 2023), banana petioles (Korram et al., 2023), chebulic myrobalan (Atchudan et al., 2023), Phlomis bracteosa (Hijazi et al., 2023), Poa pratensis (Krishnaiah et al., 2022), curaua (Raja et al., 2021), coconut petiole (Gao et al., 2021), corn cob (Jagannathan et al., 2021), lignocellulosic biomass (Qiu et al., 2022), Citrus clementina peel (Šafranko et al., 2021), betel leafs (Raja and Sundaramurthy, 2021), green pepper seeds (W. Wang et al., 2021), leftover kiwi fruit peel (Atchudan et al., 2021) via hydrothermal method. The basic schematic representation of production of BCDs using hydrothermal method is shown in Fig. 2 . Apart from hydrothermal method, solvothermal, pyrolysis, microwave and ultrasonication methods have been reported for the production of BCDs. All these methods are well described in this paper.

As a result of modern industrialization, waste chemicals are generated in abundance and disposed to the environment. Chemicals or analytes like metal ions, anions, pesticides, drug molecules, and aromatic nitrocompounds directly or indirectly pollute our environment, if present in excess amount (above WHO permitted limit), which in turn have negative effect on human as well as on animal life (Li et al., 2020; Gyanjyoti et al., 2023). Metal ions and anions such as Fe3+, Al3+, As3+, Au3+, Hg2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Ag2+, Cr6+, F−, and nitro compounds are widely used for different purposes such as industrial, domestic, agricultural, medicines, etc. However, above analytes are responsible for the issues of human heath due to their extensive presence in drinking water from the source of agricultural wastes, pharmaceutical and textile industries. They may enter the human body through drinking water, food or adsorption through the skin causing different types of diseases such as Parkinson, metabolic disorder, Alzheimer, respiratory problems, various types of cancer, hyperspasmia, gastrointestinal disorders, asthma, diarrhoea, liver and kidney problems (Li and Li, 2020; Landa et al., 2022; Dhariwal et al., 2024). Pesticides have been extensively used for a long period to protect agricultural crops from weeds, pests and also managed to treat and prevent diseases. However, excessive use and abuse of pesticides pose serious threat to the ecosystem and human health. In addition, long term consumption of foods and medicinal products with pesticides residues will cause metabolic disorders, reproductive and immune system problems (Zhang et al., 2022; Rasheed, 2023). Different drug molecules such as tetracyclines, γ-amino butyric acid, Zoledronic acid (ZA), salazosulphapyridine, morin, rutin, riboflavin, methotrexate, valsartan, dopamine, etc. are used for treating various diseases such as heart failure, Alzheimer's disease, epilepsy, cancer, brain disorders, blood disease, etc. However, some drug molecules have side effects and on disposed to water environment may lead to various other health problems (Jiang et al., 2019; J. Wang et al., 2024b; J. Xie et al., 2024; X.L. Xie et al., 2024; John et al., 2024; Kannouma et al., 2024). To overcome such problems, it is crucial to build sensor systems that can detect contaminants and prevent environmental pollution (X. Li et al., 2021). Different detection methods such as high-performance liquid chromatography, gas chromatography, mass spectrometric, voltammetry, electrochemiluminescence, surface enhanced Raman resonance, surface plasmon resonance, UV–visible absorption spectroscopy, X-fluorescence spectroscopy, atomic absorption spectroscopy, induced coupled plasma mass spectroscopy and fluorescence spectroscopy are utilized for the detection of different forms of analytes (Wahyudi et al., 2023; Chu et al., 2020; Gyanjyoti et al., 2023). In comparison to fluorescence detection system, all other methods are not suitable for industrial applications because of their complicated sample preparation process, high equipment costs and time-consuming process. BCDs can identify analytes by fluorescence response variables such as intensity, wavelength, quantum yield (QY), anisotropy, lifespan, and quenching. Such properties of BCDs make them effective nanoprobes for detection of various analytes. Various review articles on synthesis of biomass derived carbon dots and biomass based luminescent materials are reported with their multiple applications including bioimaging, sensing, catalysis, white light emitting diodes (WLEDs), anticounterfeiting, solar cells (Yuan et al., 2024; Mathew and Mathew, 2023). Although few review articles are reported on sensing applications of biomass derived carbon dots (Yuan et al., 2024; Bosu et al., 2023; Fan et al., 2022), some articles focus on particular type of analyte only, other articles are reviewed up to 2023. Therefore, these review emphases on the latest advancements of various synthetic methods to produce natural source BCDs along with their application in detection of various analytes up to 2025. The potential challenges and future perspectives of BCDs are also highlighted. This review will provide new approaches and ideas for the development and use of BCDs in future studies.

Natural resources are readily available, cost-effective, and renewable, making them a viable alternative to regularly used chemicals. Additionally, most natural materials possess intricate components, endowing the BCDs made from these renewable resources with abundance and variety. The category of plant biomass, animal products, food goods, biowastes, and waste products, which primarily include proteins, starch and lipids are suitable green and biocompatible materials which serves as the carbon sources (Ullal et al., 2022). This section explores the recent development of the several synthesis approaches for the production of BCDs. Numerous studies have been conducted on the various synthesis methods for BCDs. Each method seeks to improve the synthesis approach and optimize the reaction conditions, making the BCDs not only more cost-effective and environmentally friendly, but also to function better. The synthetic methods to produce BCDs are generally classified as top-down (cutting of large carbon precursors) or bottom-up (fusing of small precursors) (Hebbar et al., 2023). Below are the commonly used methods for the synthesis of BCDs due to their simplicity and cost-effective nature.

Among the numerous methods that had been reported for BCDs synthesis, the hydrothermal approach is the most dominating method for the synthesis of BCDs (Man et al., 2023; Kaur et al., 2025). Hydrothermal synthesis comprises a controlled hydrolysis reaction at temperatures exceeding 100 °C and pressures of 1 bar with water as solvent in a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. BCDs synthesis requires dehydration and carbonization at temperatures above 100 °C, often ranging from 180 °C to 200 °C (Krishnaiah et al., 2022; Ma et al., 2023; Seesuea et al., 2024a). Furthermore, carbonization is dependent on the type and quantity of raw materials employed. The colour variations in the precursor solution determine the duration necessary to complete the carbonization process (Zhang et al., 2019; Korram et al., 2023). BCDs solutions are typically yellow, orange, or brownish. If no colour changes are noticed, it indicates that the period is insufficient for carbonization to take place. If the black solution is obtained, the synthesis time is excessively long, and the carbon particles lose their luminous properties. The purification of the BCDs involves centrifugation at different rpm, filtration through 0.22 μm membranes, dialysis and fridge dried (Ng et al., 2021). For example, Kasirajan et al. (2024) used hydrothermal method for the synthesis of BCDs using orange extract as shown in Fig. 3 . The orange extract and aqueous NH3 solution was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. After that, the autoclave was securely closed and kept in an oven for 12 h at 180 °C. On cooling the autoclave to room temperature, brown solution of BCDs was obtained. Subsequently, the BCDs were purified by centrifugation and filtered through a filter paper to remove impurities. The resulting BCDs were stored in a refrigerator for further use. The recent development of BCDs by hydrothermal method along with their characterization techniques, structure properties has been summarised details in Table 1 .

Solvothermal synthesis means using an appropriate organic or inorganic solvent to stimulate chemical reactions of precursor molecules, which are typically put into the solution in accordance with the end-product stoichiometry and a variety of additional agents (Sahoo et al., 2020; Prayugo et al., 2023). The properly mixed liquid is then placed in an autoclave to raise the temperature over the boiling point. Temperatures are commonly ranged from 80 to 250 °C (Long et al., 2020; Wu et al., 2020). As there is a pressure build-up in the system, steel autoclaves with Teflon lining are frequently utilized to withstand pressure and reduce corrosion damage. Upon completion of synthesis, the BCDs are purified through several procedures including washing, centrifugation, dialysis and drying (Hebbar et al., 2023). The recent development of BCDs by solvothermal method along with their characterization techniques, structure properties has been summarised details in Table 2 .

It is a popular method for manufacturing BCDs from biomass. Organic substances are transformed to BCDs by heating, dehydration, degradation, and carbonization at high temperatures in either vacuum or an inert environment (Mansi and Gaurav, 2023). For instance, Chauhan et al. (2020) used thermal calcination approach for the synthesis of BCDs using coconut coir as shown in Fig. 4 . The coconut coir powder was put in a silica crucible and heated to 600 °C for 3 h in a muffle furnace with nitrogen environment. After cooling, the silica crucible containing the powder was taken out of the furnace. The resulting powder was thoroughly broken to form tiny carbon particles. Following that, finely crushed particles were further mixed with deionized water and stirred for 18 h. The recent development of BCDs by pyrolysis method along with their characterization techniques, structure properties has been summarised details in Table 3 .

The microwave method is a popular technique for direct carbonization of organic materials into BCDs using electromagnetic radiation with wavelength of 1 mm to 1 m. Because of its efficiency, simplicity of operation, and low equipment requirements, the microwave approach is a viable technique with substantial promise for large-scale manufacture of BCDs (Liu et al., 2025). For instance, Vadia et al. (2025) synthesized BCDs via the microwave treatment of Grewia asiatica fruit as shown in Fig. 5 . After washing the fruits with water and removing the seeds, pulp was collected. This pulp was dispersed in EtOH by sonication followed by heating in a microwave oven at 600 W for 15 min. Water was used for dispersing the resulting residue. Finally, the BCDs solution was obtained by sonication and filtration. The recent development of BCDs by microwave method along with their characterization techniques, structure properties has been summarised details in Table 4 .

In ultrasonic synthesis method, high ultrasonic frequencies are utilized to carry out the reaction through the thermal effect of cavitation and ultra-high frequency vibrations resulting in the formation of carbon-based nanoparticles. For instance, Zhao et al. (2019b) synthesized BCDs from soybeans using ultrasonication method. Firstly, 100 pieces of soybeans were washed with a mixture of alcohol and distilled water to remove the impurity. Then the soybeans were dispersed in 50 mL in a beaker and ultrasonicated for 2 h. During this process, the colour of the solution changed from transparent to dark yellow, indicating the peel of soybean was tailored into nanosized to form nano-biomass dots. The dark yellow solution was centrifuged at 7000 rpm for 3 min twice to remove the large size particles, after that supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μM membrane to remove the agglomerated particles. This solution is frozen at −5 °C for 6 h followed by lyophilized at −50 °C for 12 h to obtain the powders. The recent development of BCDs by ultrasonication method along with their characterization techniques, structure properties has been summarised details in has been summarised details in Table 5 . Diagrammatic illustration of production of BCDs by different methods is shown in Fig. 6 .

The structure and composition of the BCDs are mainly characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD), energy dispersive X-ray (EDX), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (John et al., 2024; Rooj and Mandal, 2023; Mahilary et al., 2024; De and Karak, 2013). The TEM analysis showed the shape of the BCDs to be spherical and its particle size in the range of 2–5 nm (Fig. 7a and b). The PXRD of the BCDs display peak at 25.320 (002) indicating the graphitic structure (Fig. 7c). In the Raman spectrum of the BCDs (Fig. 7d), two bands are observed, the D band at 1342 cm−1 due to disorder structure and the G band at 1562 cm−1 for sp2 carbon networks in the graphitic structure. The functional groups on the BCDs surface are analysis by FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. 7e). The bands observed for the BCDs were at 3310 cm−1, 2965 cm−1, 1670 cm−1, 1370 cm−1 due to –OH, -C-H, -C=O, and C-O-C functional groups respectively. The elemental and surface functional states were analysis by XPS. The XPS spectra of the BCDs exhibit two characteristic peaks: C1s (284.6 eV) & O1s (531.5 eV) (Fig. 7f). The high resolution C1s spectrum displayed three different surface components C-C/C=C (285.2 eV), CO (285.9 eV) & CO (288.8 eV) (Fig. 7g). The deconvoluted O1s spectrum produced two prominent oxygen states, including CO (532.1 eV) & CO (533.2 eV) (Fig. 7h).

The absorption of BCDs is mainly in the ultraviolet region (UV) with lower absorption in the visible and infrared region. The absorption peak at the UV region is attributed to the π-π⁎ transition of the CC bonds or n-π⁎ transition of CO bonds (Fig. 8a) (Patir and Gogoi, 2019; John et al., 2024). The functional groups on the surface of BCDs also regulate the absorption properties leading to multicolour emission (Hu et al., 2014). Fluorescence is the most investigated properties of BCDs for the purpose of applying in various fields. The most commonly observed a feature of BCDs is the excitation dependent tunable emission (Fig. 8b) (Baker and Baker, 2010; John et al., 2024). Sun et al. (2006) produced surface functionalized carbon dots (CDs) by passivating the pure CDs using two organic moieties such as polyethylene glycol (PEG) and poly (propionylethyleneimine-co-ethyleneimine) (PPEI-EI). The passivated CDs show multicolour fluorescence covering the visible and near infrared (NIR) region. Therefore, they have suggested that fluorescence properties of the CDs are not only from the particle size distribution of the samples but also from the different emissive site of the CDs. Li et al. (2010) synthesized different sizes CDs which show different emission colours. They have found that fluorescence colours of the CDs are closely related to their sizes: UV light emission (small size: 1.2 nm), visible light emission (medium size: 1.5–3 nm) and near infrared emission (large size: 3.8 nm). Ding et al. (2016) prepared tunable fluorescent water soluble CDs by hydrothermal treatment of urea and p-phenylenediamine at varied temperature ranges (140 °C-200 °C). The as synthesized CDs have similar particle size distribution and graphitic core, but surface states of the samples were different. They observed the red shift of the samples in their emission peaks from 440 nm to 625 nm due to the reduction of band gaps with increasing integration of oxygen species into their surfaces. Hence, they have concluded that tunable emission of the CDs depends on the surface groups and structures, not on the particle size distributions. Sharma et al. (2016) studied the origin of excitation dependent fluorescence in CDs based on different factors such as particle size distribution, different emissive states and in solvents system. Based on the electron microscopy results and spectroscopic data, they have endorsed the multiple discrete electronic states responsible for the excitation shifting of the emission spectra of the CDs. Moreover, surface functional groups on the CDs play important role in the extent and nature of aggregation and thus the excitation dependent or independent emission spectra. Wang et al. (2016) prepared BCDs from papaya by one step hydrothermal green approach that showed excited dependent emission, which is due to the surface defect states near the Fermi level. Song et al. (2017) synthesized BCDs from black tea which also displayed excitation dependent fluorescence. The excitation dependent emission nature of the BCDs is due to the presence of different sizes and surface states of the nanoparticles. On the other hand, Ding et al. (2017) developed hydrothermal method to prepare red emitting BCDs using lemon juice as precursor which showed excitation independent emission at 631 nm. Hence from the above results, the possible mechanisms of BCDs fluorescence may be explained includes different size distribution, different surface defects, multiple electronic states, different surface functional groups or different degree of π conjugation, as illustrated in Fig. 8c (Jaleel and Pramod, 2018; Gan et al., 2016). Further, quantum yield of the BCDs may be enhanced with elemental doping or varying the surface functional groups. Guo et al. (2021) synthesized fluorescent Cu-doped BCDs from L-trytophan and copper chloride as precursors. The quantum yield of Cu-doped BCDs increased to 9.5 % from un-doped BCDs (2.1 %). Zhang et al. (2014) synthesized blue fluorescent nitrogen doped BCDs by solid state thermal treatment. Nitrogen doping enhanced the fluorescent quantum yield of N-BCDs (31 %) significantly compared to the undoped BCDs (9 %). Liao et al. (2016) prepared water soluble fluorescent nitrogen doped BCDs via one step hydrothermal carbonization of the mixture of natural peach gum polysaccharide and ethylene diamine. The resulting N-BCDs showed enhanced fluorescent quantum yield of 28.46 % as compared to that of bare BCDs (5.31 %). Abbasi and Shakir (2022) developed nitrogen and sulphur doped BCDs using mesaconic acid, ethylenediamine and sulphuric acid as carbon, nitrogen and sulphur sources. The fluorescent quantum yield of the as synthesized N, S-BCDs was determined to be 33 %. On the other, the fluorescent quantum yield of the N-BCDs without sulphuric acid was only 13. 8 %. G. Wang et al. (2018) and M. Wang et al. (2018) proposed a method to synthesize sulphur doped BCDs by hydrothermal reaction of durian as sulphur and carbon sources in the presence platinum catalyst. The quantum yield of the S-doped BCDs reaches to 79 %. In addition, BCDs are solubility in water, good stability and photobleaching resistance. BCDs are potential fluorescence probes that can detect heavy metal ions, including biologically active, poisonous, and valuable species very efficiently (Gao et al., 2016; Sun and Lei, 2017).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing mechanism of analytes by BCDs
ce:section.ce:para: BCDs synthesis is an environmentally friendly technique that makes use of natural resources while reducing the usage of hazardous chemicals. Green synthesis methods use plant extracts and other readily available biomasses, making them a cost-effective and sustainable way to produce BCDs. Green production BCDs have specific optical properties, making them ideal for bioimaging, sensing, and catalytic applications. The sensing mechanism of BCDs primarily relies on their interaction with various analytes, leading to changes in their fluorescence characteristics. In this section a brief overview of sensing mechanism of BCDs, followed by its application of detection of various analytes are discussed.

PET is the process of transferring electrons from an electron donor (D) to an electron acceptor (A). Under light excitation, fluorophore electrons move from the HOMO to the LUMO. When an acceptor has a greater HOMO energy than the fluorophore, electrons can move from the acceptor to the fluorophore. This phenomenon is known as a-PET. The second kind is referred to as d-PET, in which electrons move from the fluorophore to the acceptor because the fluorophore LUMO is greater compared with that of the acceptor (Hu et al., 2022).

The FRET mechanism consists of two fluorophores, one of them a donor of energy and one that serves as an energy acceptor. The FRET mechanism requires two conditions that must be fulfilled. Firstly, spectral overlapping between the donor's emission and the acceptor's absorption, and secondly the strict limit of distance of 1–10 nm separating the donor and acceptor (X. Li et al., 2021).

The IFE represents a crucial fluorometric non-radiative energy transfer process that results from the absorption of emitted or stimulated radiations by fluorophoric species in the detecting system (Batool et al., 2022). IFE is also determined by the spectral overlap of the fluorophore and absorber. Unlike FRET, IFE is derived by radiative energy transfer, with energy perturbation of the donor occurring primarily at the ground state. As a result, only fluorescence quenching is observed in IFE, while the fluorescence lifetime remains unchanged (X. Li et al., 2021).

SQE is the interaction of probe and quencher to form non-fluorescent ground state complex. Static quenching can be confirmed according to the change of the quenching constant at different temperatures. In addition, the fluorescence lifetime doesn't change significantly after addition of the analytes (Liu et al., 2019). On the other hand, DQE occurs by the energetic collisions between the probe and quencher in the excited state. Hence, no changes of the absorption spectra of the probe are observed. However, the lifetime of the probe changes by this process (Gao et al., 2021).

Iron is essential metal ions in biological systems. However, high levels of Fe3+ in water, above WHO limit 5.36 mM, can lead to the formation of iron oxide, causing discoloration and affecting aquatic life. The abnormal presence of Fe3+can causes various diseases such as anaemia, heart failure and diabetes (Shander et al., 2009). As3+ is used as additives in poultry feed and agriculture. As per WHO, the presence of As3+ in natural water above 10 ppb is global environmental problem. Accumulation of As3+ in human body affects the lungs, kidneys, liver and also causes cancers (Radhakrishnan and Panneerselvam, 2018). BCDs serve as an effective tool to detect Fe3+ and As3+. Table 6 summarizes the recent development of BCDs from naturally accessible precursors, synthetic conditions, particle sizes, fluorescence, quantum yield (QY) and sensing of Fe3+ and As3+ and mechanism of detection.

Kasirajan et al. (2024) synthesized BCDs by using orange juice for the detection of Fe3+. By suppressing the fluorescence emission, the BCDs demonstrated greater sensitivity and selectivity towards Fe3+ ions than other metal ions. The limit of detection (LOD) was found to be 0.7 μM with linear range of ranging from 10 μM to 100 μM. The quenching mechanism of BCDs was attributed to the complex formation of Fe3+ and functional groups of the BCDs resulting electron transfer indicating as DQE.

Y.S. Li et al. (2024) prepared BCDs from camphor tree waste leaves through a simple one-step hydrothermal approach under mild conditions. The synthesized BCDs were found to be highly effective for detection of Fe3+ by fluorescence quenching process. The LOD of the synthesized BCDs was found to be 2.03 μM. The fluorescence quenching of the BCDs is caused by the charge transfer between BCDs and Fe3+ due to formation of non-fluorescent complex. On addition of Fe3+ to the BCDs solution, electron transfer occur between the excited state of BCDs and the d-orbital of Fe3+ is increases, resulting in activation of electron-hole pairs and reducing the fluorescence intensity of BCDs. Hence, the fluorescence quenching mechanism is assigned to be DQE (Y.S. Li et al., 2024).

Sharma and Chowdhury (2024) used Plumeria obtusa leaves for the production of BCDs via hydrothermal method. The synthesized BCDs serve as an effective tool for detecting Fe3+ by fluorescence turn off responses with LOD of 53 μM. When Fe3+ ions are added a coordinated binding occurs between the phenolic hydroxyl groups located on the surface of BCDs and Fe3+ leading to formation of stable chelate complex. This chelation process facilitates the transfer of electrons in the excited state of BCDs to the 3d orbitals of the Fe3+. This electron transfer mechanism enables non-radiative electron hole recombination resulting in decreased in fluorescence intensity.

Ma et al. (2023) produced BCDs via hydrothermal treatment of mopan persimmons. The as synthesized BCDs show blue FL with QY of 8.39 %. After selectivity study, the fluorescence of the BCDs was found to be highly selective for Fe3+ in comparison to other metal ions. A good linear range of 0–90 μM with LOD 0.324 μM was observed in the synthesized BCDs. The UV–visible and lifetime results indicate that the quenching behaviour of BCDs is due to the formation of BCDs-Fe3+ ground state complex.

Atchudan et al. (2023) utilized chebulic myrobalan to synthesize BCDs by hydrothermal approach. The obtained BCDs display excitation dependent fluorescence. The fluorescent sensing studies show the excellent sensing abilities of the BCDs towards Fe3+ ions by fluorescence turn-off process in aqueous medium with linear range of 5–25 μM and LOD is 0.86 μM. The mechanism of fluorescence quenching is due to coordination of surface functional such as hydroxyl/carboxyl groups of BCDs with Fe3+ resulting in the transfer of excited electrons from the BCDs to the outer orbital of the Fe3+ ions.

Hijazi et al. (2023) reported BCDs using Phlomis bracteosa as precursor through hydrothermal treatment. The as synthesized BCDs show blue fluorescence with QY of 57 % and found to be dependent on several factors such pH, temperature and excitation wavelength. Furthermore, the prepared BCDs were found to be good selectivity towards Fe3+ and least interference with several interfering agents with LOD of 0.041 ppm. The mechanism of fluorescence quenching is due to the transfer of excited electrons from the BCDs to the 3d orbital of the Fe3+ ions.

Krishnaiah et al. (2022) prepared BCDs from Poa pratensis by a facile hydrothermal method. The obtained BCDs showed excitation wavelength dependent fluorescence behaviour with strong cyan-blue emission upon irradiation with 365 nm UV light. The BCDs were used to detect Fe3+ and Mn2+ ions in aqueous medium with good selectivity and sensitivity by fluorescence quenching process. The LOD values of Fe3+ and Mn2+ ions respectively were calculated to be 1. 4 μM and 1.2 μM, linear range being 5 μM to 25 μM. The negatively charged BCDs leads to formation of complex with the Fe3+ by coordination which favours charge transfer between the on radiative recombination leading to fluorescence intensity diminished.

Raja et al. (2021) produced BCDs by using curaua (Ananas erectifolius) by a facile one-step hydrothermal process. The synthesized BCDs showed excitation wavelength dependent fluorescence behaviour and superior photostability. The BCDs were applied for detection of Fe3+ in aqueous medium with LOD of 0.77 μM in the linear range of 0–30 μM, a value much lower than the guideline limits proposed by the WHO. Fe3+ ions display the strongest fluorescence quenching against BCDs due to the greater affinity and quick coordination by its –COOH and –OH groups. This supports the transfer of electrons from BCDs to the half-filled 3d orbital of Fe3+ resulting in on non-radiative electron/hole recombination leads to the fluorescence quenching.

Gao et al. (2021) synthesized BCDs by using via the hydrothermal treatment of coconut petiole residues. The prepared BCDs emit strong blue fluorescence under 365 nm light irradiation with QY of 1.39 % and show favorable fluorescence stability with excitation and concentration dependent emission behaviour. Based on the mechanism of static and dynamic quenching, the BCDs were used as Fe3+ probe with detection limit of 2.3 μM in the linear range of 0.005 to 0.2 mM. The BCDs was also show the feasibility of Fe3+ detection in real water samples. The UV–Visible spectra and fluorescence decay curves studies indicate the formation of new ground state substances, supporting the static quenching explanation for the fluorescence quenching process.

Qiu et al. (2022) prepared fluorescent BCDs through the hydrothermal treatment of lignocellulosic agro-forest biomass and applied for Fe3+ detection with good linear correlation ranging from 15.6 to 62.5 μM. The fluorescence of the BCDs is quenched as the electrons of the phenolic hydroxyl groups of the BCDs are transferred to the d-orbitals of Fe3+ in the form of non-radiative electron transition.

Atchudan et al. (2019) reported BCDs from betel leaf by facile, low cost and one pot hydrothermal carbonization method. The obtained BCDs exhibit excitation dependent fluorescence behaviour, high fluorescence, solubility in water, and good biocompatibility. The BCDs were utilized as a nanoprobe for the determination of Fe3+ ions by the fluorometric method. The linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity and the concentration of Fe3+ ion ranges from 5 to 30 μM with a LOD of 0.43 μM. Highly selective and sensitive determination of Fe3+ by fluorescence quenching of BCDs was due to the resonance energy transfer mechanism.

Deng et al. (2021) applied a green microwave method to prepare BCDs from brewer's spent grain. The generated BCDs showed excellent fluorescent properties and potentials as label free probes for Fe3+ detection. The fluorescence intensity and Fe3+ concentration were observed excellent linear relationship in the range of 0.3–7 μM under the optimal conditions with LOD of 95 nM. Moreover, the fluorescent method was successfully applied for the practical Fe3+ determination in real samples such as in tap water, lake water and FBS. The fluorescence decay lifetime of the BCDs shows the values in absence (4.31 ns) and presence of Fe3+ (4.54 ns) are almost similar while the UV–Visible absorption spectra of the BCDs after addition of Fe3+ changes indicated the quenching process is SQE.

Ding et al. (2021) used crop biomasses for the production of BCDs by hydrothermal process. The obtained BCDs show blue fluorescence and highly soluble in water due to the presence of hydroxyl and carboxyl functional groups. The BCDs were used as fluorescent probe for the detection of Fe3+ with good selectivity and sensitivity, good linearity in the range from 0 to 500 μM with LOD of 5.23 μM. Due to the spectral overlap between Fe3+ and BCDs, the absorption of light weaken electron transition from valence band and conduction band, leads to fluorescence quenching of BCDs.

W. Wang et al. (2021) prepared fluorescent BCDs from green pepper seeds by hydrothermal method and applied for sensing of Fe3+ via fluorescence quenching process. The fluorescence intensity and Fe3+ ions showed a good linear relationship in the concentration range of 1 μM to 500 μM with LOD of 0.1 μM. They have found the mechanism of fluorescence sensing to be static as verified by the fluorescence lifetime studies. Further, the BCDs were evaluated as a fluorescent biosensor for the detection of Fe3+ ions in tap water and lake water samples, showing their potential value in practical applications.

Y. Wang et al. (2021) synthesized BCDs from κ-carrageenan by a facile hydrothermal treatment. The produced BCDs exhibit a high fluorescence with QY of 20.6 % and excellent stability under diverse conditions. The BCDs were utilized as dual functional sensing probe to detect both Fe3+ and oxytetracyline (Otc) respectively in compared to other metal ions and related physiological molecules. The BCDs could be used for label free sensitive and selective fluorescent detector for Fe3+ in a wide concentration range of 0–500 μM with LOD of 0.21 μM and for Otc in the range of 0–100 μM with LOD of 0.05 μM. The fluorescence lifetime of the BCDs decreases (4.40 to 3.04 ns) may be attributed to the electrons transfer from the excited state of the BCDs to the half-filled orbitals of the Fe3+ which further results in strong non-radiative electron-hole leads to aggregation induced emission quenching. In addition, the absorption peak of BCDs at 280 nm disappeared which may be relate change in the surface state of the BCDs. Moreover, the fluorescent BCDs were successfully used for Fe3+ and Otc detection in some real samples suggesting great application potential in fluorescent sensing of trace metal elements and antibiotics.

Atchudan et al. (2021) used leftover kiwi fruit peels for the production of two different types of BCDs without and with ammonium hydroxide solution via the thermal carbonization route. The fluorescence QY of BCDs-1 and BCDs-2 are determined to be 14 % and 19 % respectively. The strong fluorescence of the BCDs is selectively quenched by Fe3+ ions due to the formation of complex and the quenching behaviour showed a linear correlation with the concentrations of Fe3+. BCDs-1 and BCDs-2 showed the detection of Fe3+ ions within the concentration ranges of 5–25 μM with the LOD 0.95 μM and 0.85 μM respectively.

Zhang et al. (2019) generated luminous BCDs from crucian carp fish scales by a simple and inexpensive hydrothermal procedure. The as-prepared BCDs had significant fluorescence emissions at 430 nm, a relative QY of 6.9 %, lower cytotoxicity, substantial fluorescence stability against photobleaching, and high ionic strength. The BCDs was used as fluorescent Fe3+ nanoprobes with a linear range of 1–78 μM and a LOD of 0.54 μM. The reason for selectivity of the BCDs towards Fe3+ is the effective interaction between them via surface functional groups, which interrupts the radiative transition by electron-hole recombination, results fluorescence quenching. The suggested BCDs nanoprobes was also utilized to detect spiked Fe3+ in real water samples and human serums, with excellent recoveries and low standard deviations.

Senol and Bozkurt (2020) prepared water-soluble BCDs from seville orange (Citrus aurantium) via hydrothermal technique. The fluorescence of BCDs was found to be selective for Fe3+ ions among other metal ions and LOD was found to be 0.53 μM. The fluorescence of the BCDs is quenched due to the electron transfer from BCDs to the Fe3+. The interference studies revealed that no metal ion and anion used to affect the Fe3+ ion selectivity of the BCDs. Tests using actual samples from tap and ground water indicated that BCDs can be effectively used in real-world scenarios.

Lu et al. (2018) produced BCDs by hydrothermal carbonization of watermelon juice. The resulting BCDs with high fluorescence QY (10.6 %) demonstrated excellent water dispersibility, stable photoluminescence and minimal cytotoxicity. Furthermore, BCDs showed selective and sensitive fluorescence quenching for Fe3+ ions, with a LOD of 0.16 μM. Due to the strong coordinate interaction of Fe3+-BCDs disrupt the radiative transition, leading to fluorescence quenching. Furthermore, the CD/Fe3+ system was employed for selective sensing of cysteine based on fluorescence turn on effect with LOD of 0.27 μM.

Ran et al. (2020) used chloroplast dispersions taken from fresh spinach leaves to prepare BCDs by treating hydrothermally. The produced BCDs were homogenous and monodispersed, with an average size of 5.6 nm. It is observed that on adding Fe3+ to the original BCDs solution significantly lowered fluorescence intensity is due to the PET caused by chelation between them. At optimal conditions, the extent of the fluorescence quenching of produced BCDs was substantially dependent on the Fe3+ ions over a wide concentration range of 1.0–100.0 μM with LOD 0.3 μM.

Raveendran et al. (2019) synthesized BCDs by hydrothermally treating mint leaves. The fluorescence of the BCDs was excitation dependent and stable towards variation in the medium. The system was used as on-off-on fluorescent sensor for the selective and sensitive dual analyte identification of Fe3+ and ascorbic acid (AA). The LOD was found to be 374 nM and 79 nM for Fe3+ and AA respectively. The mechanism of AA sensing was identified by examining the behaviour of the sensor in the presence of AA derivatives indicates the interaction of hydroxyl surface functional groups.

Ngoc et al. (2024) reported BCDs from gac fruit by one step hydrothermal treatment. The BCDs was found to be highly sensitive towards Fe3+ ions in comparison to other metal ions, as evident by the quenching of fluorescence intensity and the LOD was 4.7 μM. The mechanism of fluorescence quenching is due to the charge transfer transitions between BCDs and Fe3+ via the oxygen containing functional groups.

Arumugham et al. (2020) prepared BCDs by hydrothermal carbonization of Catharanthus roseus leaves without any passivating and oxidizing agent. The BCDs have various advantages such as high fluorescence QY of 28.2 %, high aqueous solubility, stability and non-toxic. The BCDs was applied for sensing of Al3+ and Fe3+ via fluorescence enhancement and quenching process respectively. The fluorescence quenching and enhancement are due to electron transfer from BCDs to the orbitals of Fe3+ and Al3+ via complex formation. The LOD was found to be 0.5 μM with linear range ranging from 0 to 6 μM for Al3+ while LOD was found to be 0.3 μM with linear range ranging from 0 to 6 μM for Fe3+.

Tang et al. (2024) used waste of camellia fruit shell as raw materials for the production of BCDs via one step hydrothermal route. The prepared BCDs showed the presence of hydrophilic functional groups and fluorescent properties. The fluorescence of the BCDs is selectively quenched by Fe3+ ions and the quenching behaviour showed a linear correlation with the concentrations of Fe3+ from 1 to 50 μM. The fluorescence method with the BCDs was established in detecting Fe3+ in water sample with LOD of 0.75 μM. As there is a significant spectral overlap between the adsorption spectrum of Fe3+ and excitation spectrum of BCDs, the mechanism of fluorescence quenching is assigned to be IFE. Furthermore, the BCDs were used for indirect detection of absorbic acid (AA)-based fluorescence recovery.

Elizabeth et al. (2024) prepared BCDs from Morinda coreia fruits through single hydrothermal method. The as prepared BCDs showed the presence of functional groups such as carboxyl, amide etc. and emit green fluorescence with QY of 17.1 %. The fluorescent BCDs were highly sensitive to Fe3+ with LOD of 1.32 μM. This developed sensor system was applied for determination of Fe3+ ions in tap and borewell water and results showed good recovery rates. The mechanism of fluorescence sensing was due to the interaction of BCDs with Fe3+ leads to formation of non-fluorescent complex and hence static mechanism was confirmed.

C. Wang et al. (2024) produced BCDs using kiwi fruit as precursor by hydrothermal method. The obtained BCDs showed fluorescence QY of 27.85 %. The fluorescence of BCDs was quenched on addition of Fe3+ due to formation of chelate of Fe3+-BCDs and the mechanism is designated as combination of both DQE and SQE. The linear relationship between BCDs and Fe3+ was found to be 1 to 8.26 μM with LOD of 0.77 μM.

Eskalen et al. (2024) synthesized fluorescent BCDs from pomegranate peel via hydrothermal method and its QY was determined to be 7.02 %. The fluorescence intensity of the BCDs was quenched to significant extent in the presence of Fe3+ via surface functional groups of BCDs. The fluorescent BCDs were highly sensitive to Fe3+ with LOD of 7.488 μM. The performance of the sensor system was evaluated in different types of real water samples.

Arpita et al. (2024) prepared BCDs by hydrothermal method using cigarette ash as the carbon source. The BCDs show blue fluorescence which was applied for detection of Fe3+ based on fluorescence turn off process and LOD was found to be 1.1 μM. The absorption spectrum of Fe3+ overlaps with emission of BCDs facilitates the energy transfer from excited state of BCDs to the 3d orbitals of Fe3+. Hence, the mechanism of sensing is assigned as FRET process.

Y. He et al. (2024) used hydrothermal method to synthesize BCDs from green tea residue. Based on the IFE, the fluorescence intensity of the BCDs decreased significantly with the increase the concentration of Fe3+. Based on this property, linear relationship between the concentration and the fluorescence intensity of the BCDs was established in the range of 1–400 μM with LOD of 3.58 μM. Further, the probe was applied for determination of Fe3+ in actual samples with recoveries from 97.75 % -104. 59 % and relative standard deviation was 1.81 %.

Yin et al. (2024) produced BCDs by hydrothermal carbonization of folium cycas. The resulting BCDs have fluorescence QY of 8.91 %, demonstrated excellent water dispersibility and photostability. Furthermore, BCDs showed selective fluorescence quenching for Fe3+ ions in the concentration range of 0–200 μM with a LOD of 0.72 μM. Furthermore, the developed sensor system was established for detection of Fe3+ in environmental samples.

Yu et al. (2024) prepared BCDs from hakka yellow wine lees via hydrothermal technique. The fluorescence intensity of the BCDs was quenched in the presence of Fe3+ via DQE. Based on this property, linear relationship between the concentration and the fluorescence intensity of the BCDs was found to be in the range of 0.6–1.1 μM with LODs of 0.18 μM.

Zeng et al. (2024) synthesized BCDs via hydrothermal method using laurel fallen leaves as the precursor. The BCDs were applied for detection Fe3+ sensing based on fluorescence quenching effect both in water and phosphate buffer solution in the range 1–300 μM with LODs respectively were 0.34 μM and 0.48 μM. The mechanism of sensing was attributed to the IFE between the BCDs and Fe3+ via electron transfer process. Further, the as proposed fluorescent probe was successfully adopted for monitoring of Fe3+ in lake and tap water samples.

Radhakrishnan and Panneerselvam (2018) produced glutathione passivated prickly cactus fruit-based BCDs. The BCDs was highly sensitive and selective towards the detection of As3+ and ClO− ions in drinking water due to the formation of metal complexes and surface interaction with the oxygen functional groups. The detection of As3+ and ClO− ions were in the linear range of 2–12 μM and 10–90 μM with LOD of 2.3 nM and 0.016 μM respectively. Furthermore, this sensor system was explored for detection of As3+ and ClO− ions in real environmental samples for practical analysis.

George et al. (2024) synthesized BCDs from chayote seed waste by microwave technique. The BCDs show blue fluorescence with QY of 9.56 %. The fluorescence of BCDs was found to be sensitive and selective towards Fe3+ ions in compared other metal ions via PET process. Hence, these BCDs were applied for detection of Fe3+ and its LOD was found to be 4.5 μM.

Selvaraj et al. (2024) prepared BCDs via microwave heating of waste pumpkin seeds. The as prepared BCDs show blue fluorescence with QY of 9.5 % and possess good water solubility. These BCDs was used for the detection of Fe3+ based on fluorescence quenching process. The mechanism of sensing was due to the interaction of BCDs with Fe3+ to form complex via functional groups.

George et al. (2023) produced fluorescent BCDs using papaya seeds by microwave assisted method. The BCDs were used for fluorescence sensor probe for the detection of Fe3+ ions in aqueous medium with LOD of 2.35 μM. The mechanism of quenching is due to electron transfer from the BCDs to the metal d orbital, causing non-radiative electron/hole recombination leads to fluorescence quenching.

Gurung et al. (2023) reported Cynodon dactylon derived BCDs via microwave method. As prepared BCDs show fluorescence at 444 nm under the excitation wavelength of 350 nm and its QY was found to be 1 %. These BCDs are utilized for sensing of As3+ and Fe3+ respectively by fluorescence turn-on-off process with LOD of As3+ is 19 nM and LOD of Fe3+ 0.10 μM.

Ramezani et al. (2018) synthesized BCDs from quince fruit by one pot microwave treatment. The BCDs show blue fluorescence at 450 nm at excitation wavelength with a QY of 8.55 %. The fluorescence of the BCDs was quenched on addition of Fe3+ and MnO4 −. Furthermore, on addition of the As3+ to the MnO4 −, resulted in the formation of Mn2+ and followed by mixing with BCDs increases the fluorescence intensity of the BCDs in compared to the MnO4 − alone. Based on this process, As3+ was detected in the concentration range of 0.1–2 μM with LOD of 0.04 μM. Furthermore, developed sensor system was successfully applied for determination of As3+ in tape water with good recoveries in the ranges of 79–84 %.

Fu et al. (2024) produced BCDs by solvothermal carbonization of orange peels. The obtained BCDs were employed as an on-off-on fluorescent sensor for detection of Fe3+ and ascorbic acid (AA). It was found that the UV–visible adsorption spectrum of Fe3+ and excitation or emission spectra of BCDs overlap suggesting IFE. However, the Stern Volmer and lifetime observations direct the mechanism of fluorescence quenching to be SQE. The linear range of Fe3+ determination was observed in the range of 2–150 μM, with LOD of 0253 μM. Additionally, the quenched fluorescence of the BCDs/Fe3+ system was found to be recovered linearly on addition of AA with LOD of 1.57 μM.

Hg2+ is highly toxic to humans, wildlife, and aquatic life, even at low concentrations. It accumulates in food chains, causing biomagnification and is responsible for various human disorders, DNA damage, kidney toxicity and nervous system functions (Patir and Gogoi, 2018). Cd2+ is highly toxic heavy metal ion used in weapons industry, metallurgy electroplating and agriculture causes lung, kidney, bone and nervous system problems (Gu et al., 2018). Hence, it is very important to develop a sensor system to detect Hg2+and Cd2+. Table 7 summarizes the recent development of BCDs from naturally accessible precursors, synthetic conditions, particle sizes, fluorescence, quantum yield (QY), identification of Hg2+ and Cd2+ and mechanism of detection.

Watcharamongkal et al. (2024) synthesized BCDs via the hydrothermal treatment of cassava pulp at 200 °C. The synthesized BCDs were applied as fluorescent sensors for metal ion sensing. The fluorescence quenching of the BCDs observed in the presence of the several metal ions such as Hg2+, Cu2+ and Fe3+ with the LOD in micromolar levels. The paper strip coated to the BCDs could also detect these metal ions under UV light. Further, this fluorescent detection system was applied to real water samples with good recovery and acceptable relative standard deviation. The mechanism of Hg2+ and Cu2+ sensing was proposed using static and dynamic quenching methods, whereas Fe3+ sensing was proposed via IFE and either static or dynamic quenching.

Seesuea et al. (2024a) prepared BCDs from elephant manure via hydrothermal method. The BCDs exhibit blue fluorescence emission under UV light and excitation dependent fluorescence enhancement and its QY is 20.2 %. The BCDs can be used as a turn-off fluorescence sensor for detection of Hg2+ as a part of environmental monitoring. The linear range was found to be 30–500 μM with LOD 1.03 μM. Furthermore, the BCDs show antioxidant activity and promoted rice growth with an increase in characteristic aromatic compound. The schematic representation for the synthesis and detection process is shown in Fig. 9 .

Kasinathan et al. (2022) synthesized BCDs from sugarcane bagasse by ecofriendly and cost effective hydrothermal process. The BCDs was applied as cost effective fluorescent probe for detection of Hg2+ ions with LOD of 0.1 μM. The mechanism of Hg2+ sensitivity was found to be stable complex formation of Hg2+ with the BCDs, resulting in electron transfer by SQE.

Sun et al. (2020) produced BCDs by simple hydrothermal method using gardenia fruit as the precursor. The obtained BCDs display high fluorescence with QY of 10.7 %. The fluorescence of the BCDs is quenching in the presence of Hg2+ (turn-off) and the fluorescence of the BCDs can be recovered on addition of Cysteine (Cys). The linear ranges for Hg2+ and Cys were 2–20 μM and 0.1–2.0 μM respectively. Hence, these BCDs can be a promising sensor in the environmental and biological analysis.

Singh et al. (2019) used Dunaliella salina to prepare BCDs by one step hydrothermal treatment. It shows optical properties with significant fluorescence QY of 8 % and exhibit blue colour under UV light irradiation. The BCDs act as fluorescent sensor for toxic metal ions, Hg2+ and Cr6+ with LOD of 0.018 μM for both the metal ions. The mechanism of sensing is combination of IFE and DQE. Furthermore, the BCDs were successfully employed for intracellular detection of Hg2+ and Cr6+ in HEK-293 cell line.

Xie et al. (2019) utilized a green hydrothermal approach to generate fully water-soluble BCDs from highland barley. The as-synthesized BCDs have high fluorescence QY of 14.4 %. In addition, the BCDs was used for the detection of Hg2+ in the range 10–160 μM and LOD was determined to be 0.48 μM. The high efficient fluorescence quenching of BCDs by Hg2+ is due to the formation of non-fluorescent complex facilitating non-radiative electron/hole annihilation by effective electron transfer process, causing SQE.

Vandarkuzhali et al. (2018) prepared BCDs by hydrothermal treatment using pineapple peel as the carbon source. The obtained BCDs exhibit turn-off fluorescence towards Hg2+ and subsequent turn-on fluorescence on addition of L-cysteine along with enhanced biocompatibility and negligible cytotoxicity for cell imaging. The fluorescence intensity of the BCDs decreases linearly from 0.1 to 100 μM concentration of Hg2+ with LOD of 4.5 nM and the mechanism of sensing is designated as DQE. In addition, the practical applicability of BCDs was used for the quantification of Hg2+ in water.

Xavier et al. (2018) synthesized BCDs from using citron fruit extract and human urine via hydrothermal process. The turn-off-on fluorescence technique is employed for sensing of Hg2+ and cysteine. Initially, the fluorescence of the BCDs is turn off in the presence of Hg2+ via electron transfer process. It was found that on adding the cysteine to the solution, the fluorescence of the BCDs is turn on due the competitive binding of Hg2+ with cysteine. On increasing the concentration of Hg2+ from 0 to 240 μM, the fluorescence intensity of the BCDs decreases linearly and the LOD was found to be 0.15 μM. On the other hand, the fluorescence intensity of the BCDs-Hg2+ increases as the concentration of cysteine increases from 0 to 25 μM and the LOD was found to be 40 nM.

Bano et al. (2018) produced BCDs using hydrothermal treatment of Tamarindus indica leaves. The as prepared BCDs show excitation dependent emission behaviour with high QY of 46.6 %. The BCDs was used as very sensitive nanoprobe for detection of Hg2+ by fluorescence turn off process, good dynamic range from 0 to 0.1 μM with LOD of 6 nM. In addition, BCDs-Hg2+ system also act as turn-on fluorescent sensor for glutathione with good selectivity. Further, the developed sensing system was successfully applied for examining Hg2+ in pond water samples.

W. He et al. (2024) synthesized BCDs by hydrothermal method using willow leaves and sodium pyrophosphate as carbon source and modifying agent respectively. The fluorescence of the BCDs was quenched on addition of Hg2+. Based on this principle, the BCDs were applied for detection of Hg2+ in the concentration ranging from 0 to 20 μM with LOD of 9 nM. Furthermore, the generated model was successfully demonstrated for determination of Hg2+ in actual water samples with good recoveries exhibiting from 95.0 % to 105.0 % and relative standard deviation of 1.6 % to 2.8 %.

Baskaya et al. (2024) prepared BCDs from pigeon manure through hydrothermal method. The fluorescence intensity of the BCDs was quenched by Hg2+ via strong complexation of hydroxyl and carboxyl functional groups of the BCDs with the Hg2+. Hence, as obtained BCDs was applied for detecting Hg2+ with remarkable selectivity.

Keerthana et al. (2023) produced fluorescent BCDs using water amaranth leaves by hydrothermal method with a QY of 12.1 %. The surface of the BCDs was further modified with 1-pyrene carboxyaldehyde to synthesize pyrene carboxyaldehyde-BCDs. These functionalized BCDs were demonstrated for selective identification of Cd2+ over competing metal ions. The nanosensor sensing capability towards Cd2+ was found to be linear in the range 0–70 μM with LOD of 15 nM. Furthermore, the developed ratiometric sensor was used for detecting 15 nM with metal ions from water effluents.

Mohandoss et al. (2023) reported the production of BCDs by hydrothermal method using hibiscus tea extract and boric acid as the source. The BCDs show fluorescence QY of 9.2 %, high aqueous solubility, strong photostability, excitation dependent fluorescence behaviour, stability towards pH and high ionic strength. The BCDs was used for the detection of Ag+, Cr3+ and Cd2+ with LOD of 44.5, 164.4 and 54.6 nM respectively with a wide concentration range of 0–10 μM. In addition, the developed sensor system was successfully applied to detect Ag+, Cr3+ and Cd2+ in cell imaging and real water environmental samples.

Gu et al. (2018) synthesized BCDs by microwave method using scallion as the carbon source. The fluorescence QY of the obtained BCDs was determined to be 18.6 %. Furthermore, the fluorescent BCDs were used as probes for detection of Cd2+ ions via fluorescence quenching process. SQE mechanism is responsible for the Cd2+ detection as their formation of ground state complex between the BCDs and Cd2+. The linear relationship the fluorescent intensity of the BCDs versus Cd2+ concentrations were observed in the range 0.1–3.0 μM with LOD reaching to 15.0 nM.

Magnaye et al. (2021) reported the production of BCDs from Tamarindus indica and Mangifera indica by microwave technique. Both the as synthesized BCDs were found to effective for sensitive detection of Hg2+ ions. The mathematical correlation between the fluorescence intensity of BCDs and concentration of Hg2+ was wound to be a rational equation for both purified and simulated sea water. Furthermore, the generated model exhibits dynamic range between 394 ppb and 23.85 ppm.

Pineros and Oliveros (2024) prepared BCDs by microwave method using peels of palm fruit as carbon source and L-cysteine as passivating agent respectively. The BCDs exhibit high QY of 25.4 % and high solubility in water. The fluorescence of the BCDs was quenched on addition of Hg2+ with LOD of 0.19 μM. The mechanism of sensing is assigned to be static quenching.

Prayugo et al. (2023) produced nitrogen doped BCDs using jengkol peels and EDA via solvothermal method. The synthesized BCDs had a high fluorescence QY of 42 %. It was found that fluorescence intensity of BCDs reduced drastically when the Hg2+ ion concentration increases linearly from 0.5 μM to 4.5 μM with LOD of 0.03 μM. The resulting BCDs may be used for detecting Hg2+ ions in cosmetics and tap water.

Long et al. (2020) reported the preparation of dual-emissive BCDs by solvothermal treatment of cabbage. The BCDs exhibit two distinct fluorescence emissions at 500 nm and 678 nm under single excitation at 410 nm. The green emission at 500 nm had reversible linear responses to pH due to protonation and deprotonation of the surface functional groups. On the other hand, the red emission at 678 nm had efficient and selective fluorescence responses to Hg2+ by the formation of non-emissive complex between them and LOD is found to be 6.25 nM. Further, the BCDs have been successfully applied for label free ratiometric fluorometric determination of pH and Hg2+ in real fish and human serum samples with good recoveries (92.0–108.3 %).

Chauhan et al. (2020) synthesized BCDs by thermal calcination of coconut coir. The as prepared BCDs possessed QY of 48 % and displayed water solubility, excitation dependent fluorescence behaviour and stability towards different reaction medium. The BCDs was useful as sensing system with high selectivity for dual detection of Cd2+ and Cu2+ ions in aqueous medium by fluorescence on-off process with LOD of 0.18 nM and 0.28 nM respectively.

Human health may be directly impacted by environmental pollution caused by Pb2+, particularly in locations where communities are close to industrial facilities or other sources of lead pollution. Exposure of Pb2+ can result in a variety of health problems, including neurological disorders and developmental problems in children (Liu et al., 2019). Cobalt is important component of Vitamin B12 promotes human bone marrow hematopoiesis and hemoglobin synthesis. The presence of excess or deficiency of cobalt can cause various diseases (Zhou et al., 2021). BCDs can function as fluorescence sensors, detecting Pb2+and Co2+ ions in environment samples. The fluorescent characteristics of the BCDs alter in the presence of Pb2+and Co2+ ions, giving a simple and sensitive way to monitor lead and cobalt pollution. Table 8 highlights the summary of development of BCDs from naturally accessible precursors, synthetic conditions, particle sizes, fluorescence, quantum yield (QY), Pb2+and Co2+ detection and mechanism of detection.

Xing et al. (2024) prepared BCDs by a one-step hydrothermal treatment using walnut green as a carbon source and acetamide-glycolic acid as deep eutectic solvent. The as synthesized BCDs are found to be effective for sensing Pb2+ in water samples. The fluorescence intensity of the BCDs decreases as the concentration of Pb2+ vary from 0.01 μM to 1000 μM with LOD 1.55 nM and recycling rate reaches 97 %. This method has been successfully applied for detection of Pb2+ natural water bodies, indicating it's important in detection of heavy metal ions in environmental water.

Jagannathan et al. (2021) synthesized BCDs using corn cob by hydrothermal process. The as synthesized BCDs exhibited an excellent QY of 54 %. The fluorescence of BCDs is highly sensitive towards DNA, paracetamol, Pb2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, and Cr3+ indicating its multi-modal fluorescence sensor. The LOD of above analytes are 2.5446 nM, 0.8641 nM, 0.0694 μM, 1.3010 μM, 0.85504 μM and 2.8562 μM respectively.

Bandi et al. (2018) established an easy hydrothermal approach for synthesizing BCDs from Lantana camara berries. BCDs demonstrated bright fluorescence with QY as high as 33.15 % which is stable in various conditions such as heat, pH, ionic strength and under continuous irradiation of light. In addition, the BCDs demonstrated as a highly selective and sensitive fluorescence probe for Pb2+ detection. The possible detection mechanism is the efficient binding of surface groups of BCDs with Pb2+ which facilitates non-radiative electron transfer from the excited state of BCDs to the vacant d-orbitals resulting fluorescence quenching. The produced BCDs based probe demonstrated a strong linear response to Pb2+ in the concentration range of 0–200 nM, with a LOD of 9.64 nM. The probe was also demonstrated excellent accuracy without interference and was effectively used to detect Pb2+ in actual water, human serum, and urine samples.

Zhou et al. (2021) prepared BCDs from quinoa saponin and ethylenediamine by heating at 200 °C for 10 h. The obtained BCDs exhibit excitation dependent blue emission behaviour with QY of 22.2 %. The BCDs prepared displayed excellent sensitivity for Co2+ detection with good linear correlation ranging from 20 to 150 μM and LOD is 0.49 μM.

Zhao et al. (2019a) synthesized BCDs from kelp and ethylenediamine by microwave irradiation method. The fluorescence of the as synthesized BCDs was sensitive towards pH and Co2+ ion. The possible fluorescence quenching of the BCDs by the Co2+ was IFE. Under optimal conditions, the BCDs enabled selective detection of Co2+ with a linear range of 1–200 μM/L and LOD was 0.39 μM. Finally, this developed method was applied in the analysis of Co2+ in real water samples.

Z. Li et al. (2024) reported fluorescent BCDs from lemon juice by microflow irradiation technique. The fluorescence of the BCDs was stable towards pH and salt concentrations. Based on the high binding affinity towards Co2+ ions, the BCDs show good selectivity for Co2+ with LOD of 318 nM.

Chauhan et al. (2021) prepared BCDs via thermal treatment of pearl millet seeds. The BCDs has variable optical properties such as fluorescence intensity with pH, excitation wavelength and high QY of 52 %. Due to the strong fluorescence emission and optical properties of the BCDs, it was efficient for sensitive detection of Pb2+ ions by electron transfer process with LOD of 0.18 nM. The developed sensor system performed well in natural water sources with 90 % average recovery rate.

Cu2+ ions are commonly present in human body and involved in various biological process. However, long term and excess exposure to Cu2+ contributes to water pollution, affecting aquatic life and human consumption causing different diseases such as liver damage, Parkinson's and Alzheimer's diseases (Barnham and Bush, 2014). Ag+ is used in photographic, electrical and pharmaceutical industries for different purposes. However, releasing the Ag+ to the water environment after used, results in toxic effects to aquatic animals, plants and human being (Patir and Gogoi, 2024). Au3+ is used in many fields such as chemical industry, catalysis and medicine. It presence in the human body can damage kidney, liver and nervous system (Rahmani and Ghaemy, 2019). Hence, development of sensor system for Cu2+, Ag+ and Au3+detection is necessary for proper maintaining of water bodies. The various parameters like synthesis conditions, particle sizes, fluorescence, QY, LOD, linear range, is summarised in Table 9 for the detection of Cu2+, Ag+ and Au3+.

Abdullah Issa et al. (2019) synthesized BCDs using oil palm empty fruit bunches and polyethyleneimines mixture by hydrothermal process. A high fluorescent QY of 44 %, pH dependent behaviour and stability were exhibited by the synthesized BCDs. The resulting BCDs were also used as sensor to detect Cu2+ in aqueous solutions. Linear range was determined to be 1–30 μM with a LOD of 0.93 μM. The mechanism of fluorescence sensing of the BCDs was due to the thermodynamic affinity of Cu2+ for the N-chelate functional groups on the surface of the BCDs and the fast metal to ligand binding kinetics. Furthermore, this developed system was successfully applied for the detection of Cu2+ in real water.

Wang et al. (2023) reported two types of BCDs-1 (blue emissive) and BCDs-2 (green emissive) via hydrothermal treatment of Ganoderma lucidum bran. The BCDs exhibit unique excitation dependent fluorescence behaviour and high fluorescent chemical stability. The obtained BCDs were used for the detection of Cu2+. Since the lifetime of the BCDs increased in the presence of Cu2+ (1.26 to 9.15 ns) suggest the electron transfer from BCDs to the metal ion results in aggregation induced quenching. The fluorescence intensity of the both BCDs-1 and BCDs-2 decreases linearly with the increases of Cu2+ concentrations with LOD to 0.74 μM and 1.08 μM respectively.

Zhang et al. (2023) prepared BCDs via hydrothermal treatment of herbaceous Andrographis paniculata. The as obtained BCDs were used as sensors for detection of Cu2+ and L-cysteine via fluorescence turn-off and turn on respectively. Firstly, the fluorescence of the BCDs was quenched on addition of Cu2+. After adding the L-cysteine to the BCDs-Cu2+ system, the fluorescence of BCDs was recovered due the removal of Cu2+ from the BCDs surface via binding with L-cysteine. Under, optimal conditions, the LOD of these methods were 71 nM and 12 nM for Cu2+ and L-cysteine respectively. Furthermore, this developed optical sensor was verified in lake water with recovery in the range of 98.2–101.6 % and 103.3–121.6 % respectively.

J. Li et al. (2021) synthesized N, S-BCDs from grape seeds and thiourea by hydrothermal technique. The as synthesized N, S-BCDs is used as on-off-on fluorescence probe for detection of Cu2+ and ascorbic acid (AA). Under the optimal conditions, the as synthesized N, S-BCDs have good detection performance for Cu2+ and AA with linear ranges from 150 to 500 μM and 0.1–400 μM and LODs were 0.048 mM and 0.036 mM respectively.

Wu et al. (2020) reported dual and single emissive BCDs (DBCDs and SBCDs) by hydrothermal treatment of leek. Different graphitization and surface functionalization were responsible for the distinct fluorescence properties. Complexation between DBCDs and Cu2+ led to the quenching of the 676 nm emission which resulted in the ratiometric determination of Cu2+ with LOD of 0.085 μM. On the other hand, SBCDs was suitable as fluorescence nanoprobe for tetracyclines (TCs) with LODs in the ranges of 026–048 μM. Furthermore, the DBCDs and SBCDs are successfully demonstrated as fluorescent probe for determination of Cu2+ and TCs in milk and pig liver samples.

He et al. (2025) obtained BCDs from apricot leaves by hydrothermal method. These BCDs was combined with glutathione bound gold nanoclusters synthesized by reduction of chloroauric acid to form a ratiometric fluorescent probe. The BCDs serve as modulated fluorescence colour switching nanosensor to detect Cu2+. The determination of Cu2+ was performed by the visual analysis of the colour change and the intensity ratio (I450/I568) of the emission peaks. The linear range of the fluorescent probe for the Cu2+ concentration was from 0 to 120 μM and LOD was 0.65 μM. Moreover, this method was successfully applied for detection of Cu2+ in real water samples.

Jeeva et al. (2024) used pumpkin seeds to prepare BCDs via hydrothermal technique. The as prepared BCDs show blue fluorescence with QY of 12.7 %, excitation dependent emission behaviour and possess good water solubility. Out of different tested metal ions, the BCDs were found to be selective towards Cu2+. Based on this principle, the BCDs was applied for detection of Cu2+ in the linear range 0–50 μM with LOD 0.43 μM. The practical utility of the developed nanosensor was demonstrated through the analysis of Cu2+ in spiked water samples.

Su et al. (2024) synthesized BCDs from lemon peel, citric acid, 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane by one step hydrothermal method. The fluorescence intensity of the BCDs is quenched by Cu2+ concentration in the linear range 0.5–250 μM with LOD of 0.43 μM. The mechanism of fluorescence sensing is interaction of Cu2+ with BCDs by DQE. Furthermore, this developed method has been successfully demonstrated for detection of Cu2+ in water and food samples with good recoveries of 99.4–102.1 %.

Radhakrishnan et al. (2019) reported BCDs from Coccinia indica by one-pot hydrothermal technique. Further, the prepared BCDs is functionalized with different organic precursors such as L-cysteine (N, S-BCDs), ethylenediamine (N-BCDs), and glycine (N, O-BCDs), in an approached to synthesize a sensor with different binding abilities with different metal ions. The self-passivated BCDs show selectivity for Hg2+ with LOD of 3.3 nM. On the other hand, passivated (N, S-BCDs), (N-BCDs), and (N, O-BCDs) shows selectivity towards Cu2+, Pb2+ and Fe3+ with LODs of 0.045 μM, 0.27 μM and 6.2 μM respectively.

Rahmani and Ghaemy (2019) synthesized BCDs by hydrothermal treatment of mixture of gum tragacanth and ethylenediamine. The as prepared BCDs possess fluorescence QY of 66.74 % and found to be highly sensitive to excitation wavelength and pH. The resulting BCDs showed a remarkable selective quenching effect of fluorescent emission towards Au3+ ions with LOD of 2.69 μM and a linear range extended in the range of 0–100 μM. The fluorescence quenching is attributed to the coordinate bond of BCDs with Au3+ to form complexes leading to non-radiative electron transfer from BCDs to the Au3+.

Sharma et al. (2018) prepared BCDs from rose flower petals, ethylenediamine and l-cysteine mixture by hydrothermal method at 180 °C for 5 h. The BCDs show high photostability and a high selective and ultrafast turn off-on-off response towards dual S2− and Au3+ with LOD of 92.4 nM and 63.1 nM respectively. Due these excellent properties, the BCDs was explored for construction of molecular logic gates which stimulate single input logic “Yes” and multi-in put “INHIBIT” logic system. Based on fluorescence lifetime results, they have proposed that mechanism of Au3+ and S2− detection are lowering of mid-gap states and DQE respectively.

Y. Zhang et al. (2024a) produced BCDs from xylan and urea mixture via hydrothermal treatment. The as prepared BCDs were mix with cotton cellulose paper to form CPU-BCDs composite which showed excellent fluorescence properties, non-toxicity and high biocompatibility. Further, CPU-BCDs composite was highly effective for Cu2+ sensor due to its strong binding affinity of Cu2+ by coordination with O and N functional groups. The LOD was found to be 0.14 μM which was lower than US EPA safety limit of 20 μM. In addition, the CPU-BCDs composite could be used for determination of Cu2+ in real water samples with good recovery range of 90 %-119 %.

Raji et al. (2019) reported BCDs by microwave assisted method using jackfruit seeds as a green carbon source. The resulting BCDs display excellent solubility in water, high photoluminescence, QY of 17.91 %. The fluorescence intensity of BCDs was selectively quenched on addition of Au3+ may be ascribed to the affinity between Au3+ and O and N containing functional groups on the surface of the BCDs. Additionally, fluorescence lifetime measurements shows that values of BCDs remain similar in the presence of Au3+ indicates the formation of ground state complex confirming the mechanism to be SQE. Hence, the BCDs were used for Au3+ sensing in aqueous medium with LOD of 239 nM.

Zaman et al. (2021) prepared BCDs from empty fruit bunch biochar a renewable and sustainable resource using a facile microwave assisted method in just two minutes. The BCDs displayed bright blue emission on exposure to UV light irradiation. The fluorescence quenching effect was observed when Cu2+ ions were added into BCDs solution due to special coordination interaction between Cu2+ and the carboxyl and hydroxyl groups on the surface of BCDs. These contribute to intramolecular non radiative recombination of charge carriers in the excited state of BCDs to the d-orbitals of Cu2+. The experimental results showed that the prepared BCDs have sensitive response to Cu2+ ions in the concentration range of 0–400 μM.

Balakrishnan et al. (2022) synthesized BCDs from palm kernel shell by microwave technique. The BCDs display strong blue fluorescence, which is pH dependent, stability towards salt, photostability under UV light irradiation and reproducible. The developed BCDs show selective fluorescence quenching in the presence of Cu2+ ion in compared other metal ions. The fluorescence of the BCDs was quenched by the transfer of electrons via the functional groups to the metal ions. The fluorescence intensity of the BCDs decreases linearly as the concentration of the Cu2+ increases in the range from 0.1 μM to 0.5 μM with LOD of 0.05 μM.

Sanni et al. (2022) reported BCDs via microwave treatment of pine-cone biomass without any chemical additives. The BCDs were found to be highly selective fluorescence probe for Cu2+ in aqueous medium in the presence of other metal ions. The fluorescence intensity of the BCDs is quenched in the presence of Cu2+ due to interaction between them via surface functional groups. The obtained BCDs showed a good linearity in the range from 2.5 μM to 22.5 μM with LOD of Cu2+ to be 0.005 μM.

Murugan et al. (2019) produced BCDs by pyrolysis of Eleusine coracana. The BCDs exhibited bright blue fluorescence emission under UV-light irradiation. An experimental and theoretical study indicates that the fluorescence of the BCDs is strongly quenched in the presence of Cu2+ by IFE, energy or electron transfer process. Here, Cu2+ act as electron acceptor due the prescence of vacant d-orbital and BCDs act as donor by their functional groups such as π-systems, hydroxyl, carbonyl and amino groups. The quenching efficiency of BCDs versus concentration of Cu2+ is linear in the range from 0 to 100 μM. Furthermore, the BCDs have been applied in sensing of Cu2+ in real water samples with LOD of 10 nM.

Seesuea et al. (2024b) synthesized BCDs via the γ-irradiation of water hyacinth. The thiol moiety (SH) was further functionalized on the surface of the BCDs (SH-BCDs) as the turn-off fluorescent sensor for metal ions detection. The SH-BCDs display blue fluorescence and its QY was found to be 13 %. In addition, fluorescence of the BCDs is highly photo-stable and no photobleaching. The fluorescence of the SH-BCDs is sensitive towards Cu2+, Hg2+ and Fe3+ with LOD in the micromolar levels. Further, paper test strip-based SH-BCDs could be applied to detect these metal ions.

Ions containing chromium, especially Cr6+ are extremely hazardous to the environment. They have the potential to poison land and water, endangering both terrestrial and aquatic life. Cr6+ exposure can damage ecosystems, accumulate in food chains, and have a major impact on human health, including cancer and respiratory disorders (Huang et al., 2022). Fluoride (F−) in appropriate amount is used for preventing dental caries and treat osteoporosis. However, excessive intake of F− causes different health issues such as skeletal fluorosis, liver, kidney and DNA damage (Liu et al., 2021). Bioaccumulation of V5+ metal ions from Ti6Al4V implants results in reducing cellular proliferation of osteoblasts, lymphocytes and fibroblasts (Hoan et al., 2019). BCDs are fluorescent sensors that can be used to identify Cr6+, V6+and F−. Table 10 summarizes the recent development of BCDs from naturally accessible precursors, synthetic conditions, particle sizes, fluorescence, quantum yield (QY), detection of Cr6+, V6+ and F− and mechanism of detection.

Goswami et al. (2022) applied a green and facile hydrothermal method for the synthesis of fluorescent BCDs using banana stem and phosphoric acid as precursors. The prepared BCDs display excellent fluorescence properties with intense bright blue emission and the QY is found to be 15.1 %. The BCDs had sensing potential to detect Cr6+ in the linear range of 10–30 μM with LOD of 2.4 μM.
