Furthermore, the BCDs have been successfully applied to quantitatively detect Cr6+ in real water samples. Theoretical studies based on DFT and TDDFT calculation suggested that the mechanism of sensing of Cr6+ occurs via photoinduced electron transfer (PET).

Wang et al. (2022) used grapefruit juice and urea for the production of BCDs via one-pot hydrothermal method. The BCDs showed blue fluorescence with excitation wavelength behaviour and fluorescence QY was determined to be 84.93 %. Furthermore, good selectivity was noticed towards Cr6+ due the dynamic quenching of the fluorescence. The LOD was found to be 0.155 μM with linear range from 0 to 400 μM towards Cr6+. In addition, the paper sensor for the visual semi-quantitative rapid detection of Cr6+ were constructed which reveals the great potential applications as fluorescent probes for the detection of heavy metal ions in the environment.

Huang et al. (2022) synthesized BCDs via the hydrothermal treatment of poria cocos polysaccharide. The as synthesized BCDs exhibit good fluorescence stability, water solubility, anti-interference, and low cytotoxicity. Furthermore, on addition of Cr6+, the fluorescence of the BCDs is quenched due to formation of non-fluorescent complex via inner filter and static quenching effects. The fluorescence quenching of the BCDs showed a good linear relationship as the Cr6+ concentration increases from 0 to 400 μM with LOD of 0.25 μM. In addition, this fluorescent sensor system has been successfully applied for Cr6+ detection in actual water samples.

Komalavalli et al. (2020) used Hibiscus sabdariffa to produce BCDs via hydrothermal method. The resulting BCDs show blue fluorescence with excitation and size dependent luminescent behaviour. Furthermore, the BCDs were used for the detection of Cr6+ by fluorescence turn off process (Komalavalli et al., 2020).

Qiu et al. (2020) prepared BCDs from Codonopsis pilosula by a green, facile and low energy consumption method. The obtained BCDs exhibit outstanding fluorescence with QY of 12.8 % and high photostability. The fluorescence of BCDs is diminished in the presence of Cr6+ in the range of 0.03–50 μM with LOD of 15 nM. They have proposed the mechanism of fluorescence sensing of the metal ions to be IFE. The developed sensor system was applied for determination of Cr6+ in environmental water samples. Furthermore, BCDs based hydrogel sensor was fabricated for on-site semi-quantitative visual detection of Cr6+ in industrial sewage.

Hu et al. (2020) synthesized BCDs by hydrothermal method using flax straw as carbon source. The as synthesized BCDs possess fluorescence properties with high QY of 20.7 % and excitation dependent photoluminescence behaviour. The fluorescence of the BCDs turns off in the presence of Cr6+ or Co2+ based on the static quenching and IFE. Furthermore, the fluorescence turn-off in the presence of Cr6+ is turn on with addition of AA based on the reduction of Cr6+ to Cr3+ by AA. The linear ranges for the detections of Cr6+, Co2+ and AA are 0–500, 0.5–80 and 0–200 μM and their corresponding LODs are 0.38, 0.10 and 0.35 μM respectively. This sensing platform is highly sensitive to monitor Cr6+, Co2+ in real water and AA in vitamin C tablets with remarkable practicality.

Wang et al. (2020) prepared BCDs from orange peels via one step green hydrothermal method. The BCDs showed strong blue fluorescence and were further modified with EDTA. The EDTA modified BCDs exhibit higher sensitivity for Cr6+ detection via IEF with a LOD of 10 nM. The BCDs@EDTA was proved to be effective fluorescent sensor element for Cr6+ determination in real water samples with recoveries ranged from 92.09 to 104.87 %. Furthermore, BCDs@EDTA/Cr6+ system was fluorescent turn on sensor for AA with LOD of 0.1 μM. This system is also successfully applied for the analysis of AA in fresh orange and commercial orange juices with recoveries of 92.58–106.76 and 91.54–104.95 % respectively.

Zheng et al. (2021) reported BCDs from Panax notoginseng by hydrothermal treatment. The BCDs exhibits optical properties with nontoxicity and biocompatible. The BCDs was utilized as fluorescent probes for the rapid and highly selective detection of Cr6+. As they have found that the UV absorption spectrum of the Cr3+ overlap with the excitation and emission spectra of BCDs, hence mechanism of quenching of BCDs could be explained by IFE. The LOD of Cr6+ by the BCDs was found to be 0.185 nM with linear range 0–6 μM.

Roshni et al. (2019) used ground nut for the production of BCDs by cost-effective and green hydrothermal method. The BCDs display fluorescence with high QY of 17.60 %. The BCDs showed amazing selectivity towards Cr6+ in compared other metal ions. Cr6+ induced fluorescence quenching of the BCDs was recovered using humic acid and Glutathione, thus showing an on-off type sensing mechanism. The LOD for Cr6+ using the BCDs was found to be 0.1 mM.

Tai et al. (2019) synthesized BCDs by pyrolysis of shrimp shells without using any chemical reagents. The excitation dependent BCDs was found to be effective in detecting Cr6+ using the fluorescence turn off via IFE. The LOD of Cr6+ was found to 0.1 μM using these fluorescent BCDs.

Pacquiao et al. (2018) produced BCDs from enokitake mushroom by one step hydrothermal treatment in presence of diluted sulphuric acid. The BCDs exhibit blue fluorescence under UV illumination with QY of 11 % increasing to 39 % on passivating with tetraethylenepetamine. The BCDs were found to be effective for sensing Cr6+ and volatile organic compound (VOCs). The fluorescence intensity of the BCDs decreases as the concentration of Cr6+ increases from 1 to 100 μM with LOD of 0.73 mM. Furthermore, a fluorescent colorimetric paper-based system was fabricated and demonstrated to determine Cr6+ concentrations as low as 10 μM.

Y. Chen et al. (2024) synthesized BCDs via hydrothermal treatment of lignin. The BCDs show strong blue fluorescence with QY of 8.25 %. On the basis of IFE, Cr6+ effectively fluorescence quenching of the BCDs, while as potent reducing agent AA restored the quenched fluorescence, thus establishing a highly sensitive fluorescence switch sensor system for the detection of Cr6+ and AA. Furthermore, the BCDs was integrated with smartphone was successfully applied to detect Cr6+ in actual water samples and AA in various fruits.

Miao et al. (2024) prepared BCDs via the oxidative hydrothermal treatment of surgical masks. The as synthesized BCDs show dual emissive with excellent ratiometric fluorescent sensitivity for Cr6+ due to IFE and Cu corrosion inhibiting properties. The LOD of Cr6+ with these dual responsive BCDs was found to be 24 nM. In addition, corrosion inhibition efficiency of the as prepared BCDs reached to 94.01 % of Cu substrate in 3.5 wt% NaCl electrolyte with BCDs concentration of 200 μM.

J. Xie et al. (2024) produced BCDs from Lycium barbarum by one step hydrothermal method. The as obtained BCDs showed blue fluorescence under the excitation wavelength of 415 nm. Cr6+ could significantly quench the fluorescence intensity of the BCDs by DQE. Hence, BCDs showed excellent sensitivity of Cr6+ in the linear range of 0–40 μM with LOD of 0.16 μM.

Kolekar et al. (2024) synthesized BCDs via hydrothermal technique using cinnamon as the carbon source. The obtained BCDs showed outstanding optical performance for metal ion sensing applications. The developed heteroatom doped BCDs act as fluorescent probe for detection of Cr6+ and Mn7+ metal ions with LODs of 3.97 μM and 2.05 μM respectively. By studying the fluorescence decay experiments, they have found the mechanism of sensitivity of Cr6+ and Mn7+ are SQE and DQE respectively.

Patra et al. (2024) prepared BCDs by direct hydrothermal treatment of mature green tea leaves. The BCDs exhibit good water solubility, photostability, interference and low cytotoxicity. The synthesized BCDs were used for the detection of Cr6+ concentration range of 0.01–8 mM with LOD of 0.004 mM. The mechanism of fluorescence sensing is due to the formation of non-luminous complex between Cr6+ and BCDs via IFE. In addition, the developed sensor system was successfully applied to detect Cr6+ in real tap water samples.

Newar et al. (2024) synthesized BCDs by hydrothermal method using Alstonia scholaris leaves and ethylenediamine as starting materials. The as obtained BCDs exhibit better chemicals and physical properties with a relatively good QY of 21 %. The as synthesized BCDs were applied for detection of Cr6+ and Mn7+ in water samples with LODs of 0.173 μM and 0.394 μM respectively based on the FRET process.

Hoan et al. (2019) generated BCDs by hydrothermal treatment of lemon juice at 240 °C in 12 h. The prepared BCDs were found to emit bright green luminescence, exhibit excitation dependent, and photostability. The BCDs were used for detection of V5+ ions in water and in serum, based on the fluorescence quenching of BCDs. The selectivity experiments revealed that the luminescent sensor was specific for V5+ even with interference by high concentrations of other metal ions. The LOD of V5+ with the BCDs was found to be 3.2 ppm.

Liu et al. (2021) prepared BCDs by one step hydrothermal strategy from wheat straw without any additives and surface passivation. The BCDs exhibit blue florescence, special responses to F− and low cytotoxicity. A significant colour change from light yellow to red can be observed from the naked eye upon addition of F− ions to the BCDs solution due to the formation of hydrogen bonding between BCDs and F−. Furthermore, the fluorescence of BCDs also can be selectively quenched by F− with LOD of 49 μM. In addition, the BCDs are also applied for intracellular imaging and sensing of F− in living cells.

Swathi et al. (2024) synthesized BCDs from jamun seed by microwave-assisted method. The as synthesized BCDs were found to emit blue emission, water dispersion and excellent stability. The fluorescent BCDs are used for the detection of Mn7+ with LOD of 0.81 μM with a wide concentration range of 0–30 μM. The fluorescence of the BCDs is quenched in the presence of Mn7+, as there is efficient chelation between the Mn7+ and carboxyl groups of the BCDs. Furthermore, this method was applied for quantifying Mn7+ in spiked real water samples.

Das et al. (2020) prepared BCDs via the ultrasonic treatment of jute caddies followed by surface functionalization with benzalkonium chloride. The BCDs show excitation dependent emission, highly water solubility, good photostability and were utilized as a fluorescent nanosensor to detect inorganic pollutants such as Cr6+ in aqueous medium. The BCDs can detect Cr6+ concentrations as low as 0.03 μM through fluorescence quenching and further recover their fluorescence selectively for sensing AA compared with the other metal ions and biomolecules tested.

Chatzimitakos et al. (2018) reported BCDs by carbonization of human finger nails. The resultant BCDs has excellent optical properties with high fluorescence QY of 81 %. The BCDs was used as an ultrasensitive fluorescent probe for the detection of Cr6+. The LOD was found to be 0.3 nM towards Cr6+. The mechanism of fluorescence sensing of Cr6+ was combined of both inner filter and static effect.

Pesticides, widely used in modern agriculture to protect crops from weeds, pests and also managed to treat and prevent diseases, could potentially cause environmental pollution, and affect human lives. The development of BCDs based sensors for monitoring pesticides has attracted great attention in recent years (Zhang et al., 2022; Rasheed, 2023). In comparison to other fluorophores, BCDs have more promising optical features, higher QY and better biocompatibility. Table 11 summarizes the development of BCDs from naturally accessible precursors, synthetic conditions, particle sizes, fluorescence, quantum yield (QY), detection of pesticides and mechanism of detection.

J. Wang et al. (2024a) used Aloe carazo leaf to produce BCDs using hydrothermal synthetic method and applied for detection of nitenpyram insecticides. A good linear range was found to be between 0.5 μM to 200 μM with LOD of 0.15 μM. It was found that IFE process was caused by a spectrum overlapping phenomenon between the excitation spectrum of BCDs and the UV–Vis absorption of nitenpyram as shown in Fig. 10 .

Adaikalapandi et al. (2024) synthesized BCDs from bamboo stem by one step hydrothermal method. The BCDs were used for detection of fungicide dodine with LOD of 4.3 nM by fluorescence quenching process. The quenching behaviour of BCDs with the addition of dodine was examined using the Stern-Volmer equation. When BCDs bind to dodine, the Stem-Volmer plot indicates SQE due the development of a ground state complex. Additionally, the developed sensor system was used for accessing dodine in fruits and vegetables. The results showed good recovery rates from 105 to 115 % with relative standard deviation of 2 %.

Kaur et al. (2025) reported BCDs via hydrothermal treatment using Boerhavia diffusa leaves as the precursor. The BCDs were mixed with cobalt ferrite and boehmite and treated hydrothermally to prepared nanocomposite. The as prepared nanocomposite was applied as fluorescent probe for selective detection of methyl parathion pesticide and UO2 2+ by IFE with LOD 22.4 nM and 4.4 nm respectively. Furthermore, the proposed sensing platform was validated through real sample analysis.

Man et al. (2023) used broccoli leaves for the production BCDs via hydrothermal technique. The as synthesized fluorescent BCDs were used to detect O, O-dimethyl-O-2, 2-dichlorovinyl phosphate (DDVP) pesticide based on the AChE inhibition mechanism of Fe2+/BCDs system and its LOD was found to be 8.0 nM. It was found that the oxidation of Fe2+ to Fe3+ by H2O2 in situ generated from choline quenched the fluorescence of BCDs in the absence of DDVP, whereas the fluorescence of BCDs was partially or even nearly fully restored in the presence of DDVP. Furthermore, this fluorescent sensor system was demonstrated to O, O-dimethyl-O-2, 2-dichlorovinyl phosphate pesticide in food samples.

Ghosh et al. (2021a) synthesized BCDs by hydrothermal carbonization of Calotropis procera leaves without any surface passivation. The obtained BCDs exhibit excitation dependent emission with high QY of 71.95 %. The BCDs was applied for detecting isoprothiolane fungicide based on the electrostatic interaction with CN group of the BCDs that trap the surface energies, leading the fluorescence quenching. The LOD of isoprothiolane fungicide was determined to be 11.58 nM by the fluorescent BCDs. This BCDs based fluorescence approached was applied to detect isoprothiolane in fruit and rice samples.

Chen et al. (2023) produced BCDs through hydrothermal approach using crawfish shells as carbon source. The as prepared BCDs showed high fluorescence QY (18.57 %). The fluorescence of the BCDs quenched on addition of H2O2 and horseradish peroxide, resulting in detection of H2O2 with LOD of 1.25 μM. The highly reactive hydroxyl radicals produced in the interaction between H2O2 and HRP oxidized the surface reductive nitrogen, sulphur, and oxygen-containing groups of BCDs, changing their surface states and causing fluorescence quenching. PCP offered a reducing environment that can prevent oxidation of the active group of BCDs. Therefore, the addition of PCP to the system activated the fluorescence of BCDs. Accordingly, off-on fluorescence sensor was developed to detect pentachlorophenol with a LOD of 2.30 μM. Further, the developed method was successfully demonstrated to detect pentachlorophenol in three real samples. The fluorescence of the synthesized BCDs was quenched on addition of H2O2 and horseradish peroxidase (HRP) resulting in detection of H2O2 with LOD of 1.25 μM.

Vadia et al. (2023b) used Curcuma longa as carbon source to synthesized BCDs by hydrothermal process. The prepared BCDs exhibit yellow fluorescence at 548 nm upon excited at 440 nm with QY of 19.4 % and has good water stability. The obtained BCDs were utilized for detecting triazophos pesticide exhibiting favorable linearity ranging from 0.5 to 500 μM with LOD of 0.0042 μM. The average lifetime of as-synthesized BCDs was 1.91 ns, according to the lifetime analysis, whereas the decay time was 0.91 ns when triazophos was present. This changed in lifetime demonstrates the DQE due the formation of non-emissive complex at excited state. Further, this BCDs based fluorescent probe was successfully applied in water and rice samples.

Fei and Sun (2024) synthesized BCDs by hydrothermal treatment of Ginkgo biloba L. leaves and ethylene amine. The BCDs were found to be effective for sensing butocarboxim pesticide based on fluorescence turn off process via SQE. Under the optimal condition, the fluorescent sensor based on the as prepared BCDs demonstrated a good linear response for butocarboxim detection in the range from 0.625 μM to 40.0 μM with LOD of 0.2 μM. In addition, the sensor was successfully applied to butocarboxim determination in real water samples and showed good results with recoveries ranging from 97.2 to 106.8 and relative standard deviation <6 %.

Alvandi et al. (2021) prepared BCDs using greengage plums by a single step hydrothermal technique. The synthesized BCD was used for detecting multiple pesticides such as confidor, dialen super and duplosan by fluorescence quenching where their LODs was found to be approximately 10−8 ppm. They have suggested that the mechanism of fluorescence sensing of the pesticides was SQE. The prepared nanosensor does not identify the pesticides such as glyphosate, malathion, bromoxynil, diazinon and deltamethrin confirming its specificity.

Hou et al. (2020) produced BCDs by one-step hydrothermal procedure using sophora japonica leaves as the carbon source. First, the fluorescence of the BCDs was quenched by addition of Fe3+. Glyphosate was introduced to restore the fluorescence of the quenched BCDs/Fe3+ system. It results from the complexation of Fe3+/glyphosate functional groups like -H2PO3 − and –COOH. With this configuration, the turn-on fluorescence sensor based on the BCDs/Fe3+ system was able to detect glyphosate quickly. Under optimal conditions, the BCDs/Fe3+ system showed a concentration dependent fluorescent response towards glyphosate in the linear range from 0.1 to 16 ppm with LOD of 8.75 ppb. Furthermore, this BCDs/Fe3+ system was successfully applied for detection of glyphosate in real samples with good recoveries.

Chandra et al. (2022) synthesized BCDs from jatropha fruit via one pot hydrothermal process. The obtained BCDs showed blue fluorescence with QY of 13.7 %. Further, the BCDs were used as fluorometric sensor for the detection of pesticides. First, the fluorescence of the BCDs is quenched by addition of acetylcholinesterase (AChE) enzyme with controlled fluorescence quenching process. The quenched fluorescence of BCDs-AChE system was recovered on addition of chlorpyrifos. Thus, a sensitive and selective nanoprobe was developed for sensing chlorpyrifos with LOD of 2.7 nM. Additionally, the developed sensing technique has been effectively used to detect chlorpyrifos in agricultural and environmental samples with a satisfactory recovery rate.

Yang et al. (2023) used mulberry leaves for the synthesis of blue and red emissive BCDs by hydrothermal method. The BCDs were used for the detection of parathion-methyl and glyphosate pesticides by fluorescence turn-off-on process. The fluorescence BCDs was quenched as the concentration of parathion-methyl was increases from 0.3 to 65.0 μM with LOD of 0.14 μM. For the detection of glyphosate, first the fluorescence of BCDs was quenched by addition of Cu2+ ions followed by addition of glyphosate results in recovering fluorescence of BCDs. Based on this principle, the glyphosate was detected in the linear range (1.0-110 μM) with LOD of 0.60 μM. The mechanism of sensing was IFE and static quenching effect for parathion-methyl and glyphosate respectively as shown in Fig. 11 . Furthermore, the developed dual channel system was successfully applied for detecting parathion-methyl and glyphosate in food or herbal samples with acceptable recoveries.

Mei et al. (2024) developed a ratiometric fluorophore by integrating okra derived BCDs with amplex red (AR) using manganese dioxide nanosheets (MnO2 NSs) as a medium. The fluorescence intensities of the BCDs were quenched by MnO2 NSs via an IFE, whereas the fluorescence intensities of AR enhanced due to oxidation of AR to AR-Ox by the oxidase-mimetic activity of MnO2 NSs. Acetyrylcholinesterase hydrolyzed acetylthiocholine to produce thiocholine, and the decomposition of MnO2 NSs to Mn2+ by thiocholine led the fluorescence intensities recovery of BCDs but decreased the fluorescence intensities of AR-ox. Based on the above phenomenon and the inhibitory effect of dichlorvos (DDVP) on Acetyrylcholinesterase activity, ratiometric fluorophore for DDVP determination was developed. Under optimum conditions, this fluorophore gave a wide range (4–120 μM) and satisfactory recoveries (90.0–110.0 %) in food samples.

Vadia et al. (2023c) synthesized BCDs from Plumeria obtusa through hydrothermal method. The as prepared BCDs show blue fluorescence with QY of 18 %. The fluorescence intensity of the BCDs was quenched by metribuzin pesticide via a SQEs. Based on this phenomenon, the BCDs were used for metribuzin sensing in the linear range of 0.5–100 μM with LOD of 22.57 nM. In addition, this BCDs based fluorescent probe was effectively applied to detect metribuzin in the cherry tomato.

Liu et al. (2020) prepared BCDs by acid carbonization and hydrothermal treatment of pork rib bones from food waste. The fluorescence of the BCDs is quenched by dithizone ligands. With addition of dimethoate, the dithizone ligands with BCDs are replaced by hydrolysates, and fluorescence is recovered. Based on this phenomenon, the BCDs were used for the detection of dimethoate in range (0.15–5.0 μM) with LOD of 0.064 μM. Furthermore, the developed method was successfully applied for determination of dimethoate in water samples with recoveries of (93–105 %) and relative standard deviations were (2–6 %).

Ghosh et al. (2021b) used Tagetes erecta to produce BCDs via hydrothermal treatment. The as synthesized BCDs show strong blue fluorescence with QY of 63.7 %. Moreover, on addition of chlorpyrifos, the fluorescence of the BCDs was turn off while the fluorescence of the BCDs was turn on with addition of quinalphos. The fluorescence signal of the BCDs changes showed good linearity with chlorpyrifos and quinalphos concentrations in the range of 0.05–100.0 μM for chlorpyrifos and 0.01–50.0 μM for quinalphos with LODs of 2.1 nM and 1.7 nM respectively. Furthermore, the BCDs based fluorescent nanoprobe was successfully applied to estimate chlorpyrifos and quinalphos with effective accuracy in rice and fruit samples.

Devi et al. (2023) reported BCDs by hydrothermal method using walnut shell as the starting materials. The as obtained fluorescent BCDs were applied for detection of imidacloprid by fluorescence turn on process. During the addition of imidacloprid to the BCDs solution, ground state complex is formed between them due to the interaction of functional groups on the surface of BCDs and imidacloprid, where emission wavelength is blue shift indicating the association with ability to donate protons. Further, the presence of imidacloprid in the BCDs solution reduced the self-aggregation due to strong binding between them resulting excited state intramolecular proton transfer (ESIPT) process. Under optimized experimental conditions, the linear range of imidacloprid was 0–80 μM with LOD of 7.58 μM.

Q. Zhang et al. (2024) synthesized BCDs from Lycium ruthenicum by hydrothermal treatment. The obtained BCDs show blue fluorescence with QY of 10.63 %, excellent photostability and no-toxic properties. It was found that the fluorescence intensity of the BCDs was quenched on addition of sunset yellow (SY). Based on this observation, the BCDs fluorescent sensor was developed for the determination of sunset yellow in the concentration of 0.05 to 35.0 μM, with LOD of 17 nM. The fluorescence lifetime of the BCDs and BCDs/SY were found to be 5.75 ns and 5.61 ns respectively, indicating the fluorescence quenching mechanism was SQE. On further investigation, reveal that the excitation and emission spectra of BCDs overlap with the spectrum of SY designating the involvement of IFE. Hence, the detection mechanism of SY attributed to both SQE and IFE. This fluorescent sensor system was also applied for determination of sunset yellow in practical drink samples and show satisfactory results.

Zhu et al. (2024) used pomelo peels to prepare BCDs by hydrothermal method. The as prepared BCDs show bright blue fluorescence with QY of 3.63 %, excellent photostability, low toxicity and high solubility. It was found that two widely used synthetic food colorants, tartrazine and sunset yellow could quench the fluorescence of the BCDs. Based on this property, an analytical method for the determination of tartrazine and sunset yellow was developed using BCDs as fluorescent probe. A linear relationship was found in the range of 0–100 μM for tartrazine and sunset yellow with LODs of 0.65 nM and 1.7 nM. The mechanism of sensing was both via IFE and SQE. Furthermore, the developed method was successfully applied for determination of tartrazine and sunset yellow in food samples collected from local markets and the recovery rates of the two ranges from (79–117.8 %) and 81–103.5 % respectively.

Garg et al. (2024) prepared BCDs from Hibiscus rosa-sinensis leaves by hydrothermal technique. The as synthesized BCDs show bright red fluorescence with QY of 56 %, excitation dependent emission, stability under ionic strength and pH. The fluorescence emission behaviour of BCDs displayed selective turn off fluorescence response to tartrazine over the interfering species with LOD of 0.09 μM. The carboxyl or hydroxyl functional groups on the surface of the BCDs and sulphonic groups of dyes form complex via hydrogen bonding which results in fluorescence quenching.

Osman et al. (2024) synthesized BCDs from Medicago sativa L. (alfalfa) by microwave method. The BCDs show blue fluorescence with QY of 2.2 % and high-water solubility. The BCDs were employed to quantify the antibacterial drug, nifuroxazide (NFZ), by fluorescence quenching via IFE mechanism. The method was linear from 1.0 to 20.0 μM with LOD of 0.16 μM. Further, the fluorescent sensor system was applied for determination of NFZ in gastric juice and pharmaceutical dosage with good recoveries at 99.59 % and 100.6 % respectively.

Vadia et al. (2025) developed BCDs via microwave assisted method using Grewia asiatica fruits as a source. The as obtained BCDs exhibit red fluorescence with QY of 21 %. The emission intensity of BCDs is selectively quenched by quinalphos pesticide via IFE. The BCDs serve as a versatile turn off fluorescent probe for quinalphos pesticide sensing in the linear range of 0.25–60 μM with LOD of 0.00159 μM. The integration of BCDs with fluorescence spectroscopy offers to establish a rapid, simple and miniaturize analytical strategy for quinalphos pesticide assay in vegetable, water and soil samples.

Vadia et al. (2023a) produced BCDs via carbonization of lemon peel. The as prepared BCDs display blue fluorescence at 468 nm under the excitation wavelength of 378 nm with QY of 32 %. Firstly, the fluorescence of BCDs was turning off by addition of Fe3+. In the second step, this BCDs/Fe3+ system was applied for sensing of propiconazole (PC) by fluorescent turn on process in the linear range of 0.1–40 μM with the LOD of 0.054 μM. Furthermore, the BCDs have effectively applied for detection of PC in pharmaceutical and vegetable samples.

Different nitrocompounds such as 4-Nitrophenol, picric acid etc. are manufacture in industry for different purposes such as industrial products like agrochemical products, explosives, pesticides, textiles, leather and pharmaceuticals etc. However, it could lead to water and soil pollution if discharged into environment which in turn may causes various human health issues such as headache, dizziness, nausea and vomiting etc. (Ashokan and Bhunia, 2024; Patir et al., 2021). Therefore, it is of great significance to develop a simple, highly sensitive and fast responding method for detecting nitrocompounds in water. Table 12 summarizes the development of BCDs from naturally accessible precursors, synthetic conditions, particle sizes, fluorescence, quantum yield (QY), detection of nitrocompounds and mechanism of detection.

Li et al. (2018) prepared fluorescent BCDs by hydrothermal method using grape skin as the precursor and applied for detection of picric acid by fluorescence turn off process. Hence, it was used as a sensor for picric acid in the linear range of 0.06–79.4 μM with LOD of 10 nM. Both the phenolic hydroxyl groups on the picric acid and the carboxyl groups on the BCDs are capable of dehydration and condensation. Additionally, the nitro group on PA, received an electron from the amino group on BCDs. Fluorescence quenching resulted from these processes, which sped up the excitation's non-radiative electron/hole recombination. The method was successfully applied for the detection of picric acid in real samples with good recoveries (95.64–104.2 %) with relative standard deviation (RSD) of (0.26–0.74 %).

Liu et al. (2025) used pinecone as the precursor to produced BCDs via hydrothermal technique. The BCDs show excitation dependent emission behaviour and photostability. The fluorescence intensity of the BCDs was quenched by trinitrophenol (TNP). The probe exhibited linear range of 0.025–20 μM with LOD of 8.5 nM. The results showed that the presence of TNP had virtually no effect on the fluorescence lifetime of BCDs, which was 4.06 ns, while the fluorescence decay curves of BCDs and TNP showed no discernible fluctuations, indicating a strong effective IFE between CDs and TNP. In addition, the developed method proved successful in sensing of TNP quantitatively in actual environmental samples with good recoveries of 95.6–99.6 %.

Koprade et al. (2024) synthesized BCDs by reflux method using okra peels as the source. The BCDs emit blue fluorescence with QY of 12.0 % and excitation dependent behaviour. Further, the BCDs were employed as fluorescence nanoprobe for detection of hazardous metal ions and nitrocompounds. The fluorescence of the BCDs was quenched on addition of the analytes Cr6+, Mn7+, 4-nitroaniline (4-NA) and picric acid (PA). Based on this fluorescence quenching effect, the LODs of Cr6+, Mn7+, 4-NA and PA are 1.46 μM, 1.05 μM, 2.1 μM and 2.2 μM respectively. The produced BCDs had a high CC content, which suggests that they will interact with the Mn7+ ion, which in aqueous conditions takes the form of MnO4, undergo a redox process, and yield MnO2. The development of ground state complexes and quenching of florescence are caused by the alkene group in BCDs reacting with MnO4 and reducing to MnO2. A degradation value for the fluorescence life time of roughly 1 indicates SQE. When 4-NA is present, an absorbance spectrum that coincides with the emission spectra of BCDs indicates secondary IFE. The primary IFE is suggested by the PA absorbance spectra overlapping the BCDs excitation spectra. This finding implied that the quenching of 4-NA and PA was entirely due to static quenching and IFE. Furthermore, this method was successfully applied for detecting above analytes in real water samples.

Guo et al. (2024) produced BCDs by hydrothermal treatment of bell pepper. The as synthesized BCDs emit blue fluorescence with QY of 23.2 %. The BCDs serve as pH modulated fluorescence switching nano-sensor to detection 4-NP from 0.054 to 68 μM with LOD of 54 nM. There is significant overlap between the excitation spectrum of BCDs at 401 nm and the absorption spectrum of 4-NP at 399 nm which suggested that IFE might be the main cause of the quenching of fluorescence. At the same time, there was an insignificant change in the average lifetime of BCDs when 4-NP was present, confirming the IFE-induced quenching effect between BCDs and 4-NP. Moreover, this method was successfully applied in practical samples with satisfactory recovery of 101.8–107.5 %. Furthermore, a smartphone integrated optical sensing device with BCDs based film was developed for detecting 4-NP with LOD 0f 0110 μM. In addition, this device was validated in crop samples with good recovery of 101.6–108.6 %.

Zulfajri et al. (2020) prepared BCDs by a facile hydrothermal method using oyster mushroom as a carbon source. The obtained BCDs show cyan colour fluorescence with QY of 12.51 %. The fluorescence of the BCDs was found to be excitation dependent and effective as fluorescence sensor for nitroarenes (NAs) with wide linear responses and low detection limits. The mechanism of fluorescence sensing was found to be combination of IFE, FRET and PET. Finally, this fluorescent sensor system was used for determination of NAs in real water samples with spiked recoveries in the range of (97.20–1-2.60 %) with relative standard deviation below 2 %.

Michenzi et al. (2024) synthesized fluorescent BCDs via hydrothermal treatment of orange peel waste and its QY was determined as 2.2 %. The BCDs serve as fluorescence nano-sensor to detect nitrobenzene from 0.16 to 36.6 μM with LOD of 14 μM based on both transient and dynamic quenching effect between the BCDs and nitrobenzene.

Venugopalan and Vidya (2023) used microwave pyrolysis method to produced BCDs from sweet flag (Acorus calamus). The obtained BCDs show excitation dependent fluorescence behaviour. The fluorescence of the BCDs was quenched when exposed to 4-nitrophenol (4-NP). Due to this phenomenon, the BCDs proposed as probes to detect the 4-NP in the linear range of 0–14.28 μM with LOD of 0.207 μM. They found that when the electron-rich amino group on the surface of BCDs interacts with the electron-deficient 4-NP, which is caused by the strong electron-withdrawing effect of the nitro group at the para position, a non-emissive complex is formed. Through electron transfer-induced fluorescence quenching, this complex formation may result in the dampening of fluorescence. Additionally, overlap between the absorption spectra of 4-NP and both excitation and emission spectra of BCDs were noted, confirming that IFE and SQE were the detection mechanisms. In addition, a practical application of the developed technique was demonstrated to detect 4-NP in natural water resources with good recovery percentages.

Chatzimarkou et al. (2018) applied a single one-step pyrolysis method to synthesize BCDs using apple seeds as the carbon source. It was found that fluorescence intensities of BCDs were quenched by 4-NP through FRET mechanism. The linear range was found to be 0.05–53.0 μM, with LOD of 13 nM. Furthermore, this BCDs based nanoprobe was successfully applied as analytical method for detection of 4-NP in environmental water samples and human urine.

Different drug molecules such as tetracyclines, γ-amino butyric acid, Zoledronic acid, uric acid, salazosulphapyridine, morin, rutin, riboflavin, methotrexate, valsartan, dopamine etc. are used for treating various diseases such as heart failure, Alzheimer's disease, epilepsy, cancer, brain disorders, blood disease etc. However, some drug molecules have side effects and on disposed to water environment may leads to various other health problems as per the below discussed articles. Therefore, developing highly sensitive and fast responding method for detecting drug molecules in water is necessary. Table 13 summarizes the development of BCDs from naturally accessible precursors, synthetic conditions, particle sizes, fluorescence, quantum yield (QY), detection of drug molecules and mechanism of detection.

Zhao et al. (2018) synthesized BCDs by hydrothermal method using pork as the carbon source. The BCDs have high fluorescent QY of 17.3 % and satisfactory chemical stability. It was found that when uric acid was added, the fluorescence intensity of BCDs was quenched. Based on this phenomenon, uric acid was detected using the fluorescence technique with a LOD of 0.05 μM in the linear range of 0.1–100 μM. It was discovered that SQE is the uric acid detecting mechanism which is due to the formation of ground sate complex and aggregation of BCDs. Lastly, the proposed method was used with good results to determine the amount of uric acid in human serum and urine samples.

Sangubotla and Kim (2019) produced BCDs from corn juice by hydrothermal process. Further, the BCDs was functionalized with 3-aminophenylboronic acid and nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide phosphate (NADP+) via 1-ethyl-3(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide hydrochloride/N-Hydroxy-succinimide coupling reaction termed as CANs. This functionalized BCDs (CANs) were employed for detection of γ-aminobutyric acid (GABA) by fluorescence quenching via electron transfer between enzyme and substrate due to formation of reduced NADPH. The proposed method was used for detecting GABA in the linear range of 0–90 μM with LOD of 6.46 μM. Furthermore, this sensor system was applied for determination of GABA in biological samples with recovery results of 93.2–104.6 %.

Jia et al. (2023) prepared BCDs via hydrothermal method using pitaya peel and ethylenediamine as the precursors. The as synthesized BCDs was used as multifunctional nanosensor for the detection of tetracycline (TC), oxytetracycline (OTC) and chlortetracycline (CTC) based on IFE, static quenching and electrostatic interaction. The linear ranges were found to be 1–60 μM, 1–40 μM, 1–80 μM, respectively for TC, OTC and CTC with LOD of 33.8, 40.5 and 41.9 nM respectively. In addition, fluorescence sensor array was developed for identifying multiple TCs in real samples.

Amin et al. (2018) synthesized BCDs via the hydrothermal treatment of date kernel. The BCDs exhibited excellent water solubility, ionic and photo-stable and a have high fluorescence QY of 12.5 %. In presence of Fe3+, the fluorescence intensity of BCDs was quenched due to interaction between Fe3+ and the functional groups of the BCDs (turn-off). After that, on addition of the Zoledronic acid (ZA), the fluorescence of the BCDs turn-on due to the dominance of phosphate groups of ZA interacting with functional groups on the surface of BCDs in compared to Fe3+. Under optimal conditions, the proposed fluorescence sensor (CD-Fe3+) shown good sensing performance of ZA in the linear range of 0.1–10 μM with LOD of 0.04 μM. Furthermore, the developed CD-Fe3+ sensor system was successfully used for detection of ZA in human serum samples with good recoveries.

Jiang et al. (2019) used ginkgo leaves as a source for the production of BCDs via hydrothermal technique. The obtained BCDs have strong blue fluorescence, excitation dependent emission and QY of 22.8 %. The fluorescence of BCDs was quenched on addition of salazosulphapyridine (SASP). As the concentration of SASP was increased from 1 to 80 μM, there was linear relationship with LOD of 40 nM. Based on the UV–visible and fluorescence decay results, the mechanism of sensing was assigned as the combination of IFE and SQE. This method was successfully applied to detect SASP in mouse plasma with recovery range 96 %–101 % and relative standard deviation from 2.6 %-3.1 %.

Shahshahanipour et al. (2019) prepared BCDs by hydrothermal method using Lawsonia inermis (Henna) plant as a carbon source with adding any chemical reagents. The as prepared BCDs were used as biocompatible probe for determination of methotrexate (MTX) in the range of 0.02–10 μM with LOD of 7 nM/L. The MTX molecules and CDs are separated by roughly 3 nm, and every circumstance is in good agreement with the ideas of Förster's non-radioactive energy transfer theory. In this assay, MTX serves as the quencher of the fluorescence emission of BCDs. Moreover, their absorption and emission spectra reveal that MTX molecules have a broad absorption spectrum that overlaps with the emission spectra of BCDs. All these Factors reveal that the sensing mechanism is FRET.

Raju et al. (2024) produced BCDs from drumsticks (Moringa oleifera) via hydrothermal treatment. The BCDs were explored as fluorescent sensor for detection of H2O2 and aspartic acid by fluorescence on-off-on process. It was found that the fluorescence intensity of the BCDs was quenched successively on increasing the concentration of H2O2 with LOD of 26.4 mM. The nitrogen and oxygen-rich surface of the synthesized BCDs makes it easier for the FRET mechanism to transfer electrons from the BCDs to H2O2, which ultimately leads to fluorescence quenching. Due to FRET mechanism the electrons get transferred from the conduction bands of BCDs to H2O2, when it comes into contact with them. Under the influence of reactive oxygen species H2O2, L-aspartic acid was readily oxidized, which restored or “turned on” the fluorescence intensity of BCDs. Because of the cleavage of FRET pairs and the free BCDs, the FRET process is hindered, increasing the intensity of quenched fluorescence. The recovery of the quenched fluorescence intensity of BCDs was observed on increasing of concentration of aspartic acid with LOD of 134.2 nM.

Ma et al. (2024) used hydrothermal method to prepare BCDs using discarded chicken feathers and ammonium chloride as precursors. The as prepared BCDs were used as fluorescent nanosensor for the efficient qualitative and quantitative detection of CTC in samples. The fluorescence quenching responses of the BCDs with respect to the concentration of CTC was found to be linear in the ranges 0.05–0.35 mM. They found that CTC has a wide UV–visible absorption band, and there was a noticeable overlap between its UV–visible absorption spectra and the excitation spectrum of BCDs. Beyond that, the overlap of UV absorption bands can trigger electron transfer. Charge is transferred from the conduction band of BCDs to the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital of CTC via the overlap between the UV–visible absorption spectra of CTC and BCDs, which causes fluorescence quenching. All those factors led to the conclusion that mechanism of sensing was due to SQE and IFE between CTC and BCDs. In addition, fluorescence sensor array was developed for identifying CTC in real samples by spiked recovery experiment.

Chahnasir et al. (2024) synthesized BCD@EDTA/MOF-199 nanohybrid via hydrothermal method using orange peels, EDTA and Cu-based metal organic framework (MOF-199) as precursors. During fluorometric test, BCD@EDTA/MOF-199 nanohybrid dramatically and specifically turned on the fluorescence towards L-AA determination. Under ideal circumstances, the probe displayed a satisfactory linear response in the concentration range of 10 nM-100 μM with LOD of 8.6 nM. Further, BCD@EDTA/MOF-199/L-AA system was applied as fluorescent on-off sensor for Fe3+ determination with LOD of 1.15 μM.

John et al. (2024) prepared BCDs from Acorus calamus via hydrothermal process. The obtained BCDs showed blue fluorescence with QY of 15 %. Based on fluorescence quenching, ratiometric nanosensor was developed to detect morin in the linear range of 0–2 μM, with LOD of 96 nM. They observed that there was no noticeable difference in the fluorescence lifetime both prior to and following the addition of morin solution to the BCDs. This finding suggested the possibility of a SQE rather than the DQE between CQDs and morin. Further evidence for the potential of a SQE comes from the BCDs-morin mixture's absorption spectra when compared to those of pure BCDs. Also, the spectral overlapping between the absorption spectrum of morin and BCDs suggesting the mechanism of detection of morin is the combination of SQE and IFE.

J. Wang et al. (2024b) used corn stover as a source for the production of BCDs via hydrothermal method. The BCDs have strong fluorescence with QY of 37.4 %. The fluorescence of BCDs was quenched on addition of quercetin. The linear relationship between the BCDs emission intensity and quercetin was observable from 10 to 200 μM, with LOD of 6.41 μM. The mechanism of the fluorescence sensing was due to the inner filter and static quenching effect between the BCDs and quercetin.

X.L. Xie et al. (2024) applied hydrothermal method to prepared BCDs from cumin seeds. The as synthesized BCDs have strong fluorescence with QY of 10.60 %. Rutin, a natural active component could quench the fluorescence of the BCDs through IFE. The linear relationship between the BCDs emission intensity and rutin was observable from 0.05 to 30 μM, with LOD of 0.015 μM. Further, the developed nanosensor system was applied for detecting rutin in Flos Sophorae, rutin tablets and capsules.

J. Chen et al. (2024) synthesized BCDs by hydrothermal method using bird's nest as the carbon source. The BCDs showed bright green emission, water soluble, stable towards pH, ionic strength and light. The experiment revealed that the fluorescence of the BCDs showed regular decrease after the interaction of BCDs with vitamin B12 in the linear range of 0–100 μM, with LOD of 0.24 μM. The structural analysis and fluorescence lifetime decay results indicate the transfer of electrons from BCDs to the Vitamin B12 leading to fluorescence quenching by DQE. Finally, the proposed method was applied for detecting vitamin B12 in human serum samples with recoveries of 96.2–100.3 %.

Han et al. (2024) prepared fluorescent BCDs with QY of 35.37 % by hydrothermal method using orange peel as the carbon source. The fluorescence of the BCDs found to be quenched on addition of dopamine due to π-π interactions between the intermolecular aromatic rings. Based on this process, fluorescent probe was developed to detect dopamine where the linear range was from 0 to 300 μM, with LOD of 0.168 μM. Further, the developed method was demonstrated as suitable sensor for dopamine in rabbit serum samples with recoveries of 98–103 % and relative standard deviation of 2–3 %.

Monte-Filho et al. (2019) developed BCDs from lemon and onion biomasses by a facile, fast and low-cost one-step microwave-assisted carbonization method and applied for detection of riboflavin. The as prepared BCDs show good optical properties, high QY (23.6 %) and excellent water solubility. The detection of riboflavin was caused by the FRET mechanism between the electron-accepting riboflavin and the electron-donating BCDs. The >C=O and -NH groups in riboflavin strongly attract the hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the surface of BCDs, forming hydrogen bonds and reducing the spatial separation between the two, which effectively helps the BCDs/riboflavin function as an efficient FRET pair. The linear relationship between BCDs and riboflavin is observed in the range from 0.10 to 3.0 μM with LOD of 1.0 nM. Furthermore, this analytical method was used for determination of riboflavin in multivitamins or mineral supplements with good recoveries (96.0 % -101. 4 %).

Kaur et al. (2024) used Indian gooseberry (Phyllanthus emblica) as precursor to produced BCDs by microwave method. The BCDs have bright green emission, water soluble, stable towards pH, ionic strength, light and fluorescence QY of 48 %. In addition, the BCDs selectively detect riboflavin (vitamin B2) in aqueous medium in compared to others bio-analytes with LOD of 35 μM. Further, the nanosensor was found to be effective for detecting vitamin B2 present in commercially available pharmaceutical tablets with a LOD of 61 μM. The mechanism of sensing was assigned as the FRET between vitamin B2 and BCDs interfaces.

Kannouma et al. (2024) produced BCDs via microwave method using eggshell waste, EDTA and glycerol as precursors. Molnupiravir was found to quench the native fluorescence of the BCDs in quantitative manner, based on a static quenching mechanism by forming a non-emissive complex. The proposed fluorescent probe showed linearity for molnupiravir detection over a range of 0.05–0.35 mM, with recoveries ranging from 99.53 % to 101.79 % and relative standard deviation was <1 %. Further, developed fluorescent sensor was successfully employed to determine molnupiravir levels in marketed hard capsules with good recoveries percentage. The mechanism of sensing was due to static and IFE between CTC and BCDs.

D. Zhang et al. (2024) synthesized BCDs from fruit shells by solvothermal treatment. The BCDs was mixed with methacrylic acid, azobis ethylene glycol dimethacrylate and 2-methylpropionitrile followed by reacted under optimal conditions to form the BCDs@MIPs. This final product BCDs@MIPs was useful as sensor for detection of tyramine in the linear range of 1–60 μM with LOD of 1.3 μM. In addition, this analytical method was used for determination of tyramine in real samples with recoveries (79.74 % -112.12 %). Additionally, tyramine can be determined via smartphone-based fluorescence signal identification analysis, which offers a portable visual optical analysis for quick on-site testing.

Y. Zhang et al. (2024b) prepared BCDs via the thermal treatment of grass carp fish scale. The BCDs exhibit good water solubility, biocompatibility and excellent optical properties with high fluorescence QY of 23.8 %. On addition of Ag+, the fluorescence of the BCDs is quenched due to electron transfer between Ag+ and BCDs. It is probable that the excited BCDs moved electrons to the Ag+ d-orbital to create the complex of BCDs-Ag+, which quenched the fluorescence intensity of BCDs. Glutathione and Ag+ established an AgS bond after being added to the BCDs-Ag+ solution, which caused the complex to dissociate and restore fluorescence of BCDs. Based on this phenomenon, glutathione was detected in wide linear range (1.6–36.0 μg/mL) with LOD of 0.77 μM. The summary chart of synthesis, properties and sensing application of the BCDs are as shown in Fig. 12 .

This paper reviewed the synthesis of carbon dots from different bio-sources such as plant extracts, animal extracts, waste materials and food items. BCDs were mainly prepared by hydrothermal, solvothermal, microwave, pyrolysis and ultra-sonication methods. Characterization and optical properties of the BCDs are discussed with the help of various analytical instruments such as TEM, SEM, EDX, XRD, UV–Visible and fluorescence spectroscopy. Based on the fluorescent properties, the BCDs were explored for sensing of analytes like metal ions, anions, pesticides, drug molecules, and aromatic nitrocompounds release from different sources such as agricultural wastes, pharmaceutical and textile industries, directly or indirectly pollute our environment, which in turn have negative effects on human as well as on animal life. Their distinctive features, including superb fluorescence, low toxicity, biocompatibility, and ecologically benign production, make them appealing for a variety of applications. However, significant obstacles remain in realizing their full potential, including concerns about consistent synthesis, functionalization, sensitivity, selectivity, stability, and scalability. Overcoming these difficulties require a collaborative effort to advance synthesis processes, functionalization tactics, composite material development, and environmentally friendly production methods.

Despite the fact that BCDs are more environmentally friendly by nature than those derived from chemical precursors, more work is required to reach their full potential. Utilizing food scraps, forestry leftovers, and agricultural waste offers a special chance to increase the value of wasted goods. However, the naturally fluctuating content of biomass feed stocks can make it difficult to synthesize BCDs with reliable characteristics. Research is moving towards environmentally friendly synthetic methods that are reliable and scalable. Because of their ease of use and very low energy usage, hydrothermal and microwave-assisted synthesis techniques are especially promising. To increase yield, homogeneity, and purity, future research may investigate solvent-free pathways and template-assisted techniques. Also, adopting sustainable synthesis processes could decrease the use of hazardous chemicals and solvents. Optimization could involve using water as solvent, biodegradable precursors, and improving reaction conditions to minimize waste. Thorough lifespan studies can assist examining the ecological impact of BCDs from production to disposal, assuring sustainable, eco-friendly procedures and the possibility of recycling or properly disposing of the BCDs after usage.

The exact control of the optical characteristics of BCDs is one of the most important topics for further investigation. Because of their tunable fluorescence which frequently ranges from blue to near-infrared, these nanomaterials are very appealing for use in bioimaging, optoelectronics, and sensing. Investigations into the mechanisms governing their emission are still ongoing. Fluorescence of BCD is thought to originate from both carbon core and surface states. It should be mentioned that the synthetic conditions and biomass precursor selection significantly alter the physical as well as optical properties of BCDs. Hence, future studies might concentrate on defining distinct correlation between structure and properties of BCDs. This includes exploring how fluorescence is affected by atomic composition, graphitization level, and the presence of heteroatoms like N, S, B, P, etc. However, researchers are actively examining different doping strategies, which include intentionally adding elements into the carbon lattice to modify the emission wavelength, improve QY and allow excitation-independent emissions.

Moreover, BCDs have shown a great deal of potential in cellular imaging because of their adjustable fluorescence emission. Due to their nanoscale size, they can readily pass through cell membranes and settle in cellular sites like the nucleus or cytoplasm. Real-time imaging of cellular structures is made possible by the intense fluorescence of BCDs. A broad spectrum of wavelengths, including the NIR area, can be emitted by BCDs through surface passivation and controlled doping. Because it reduces light scattering and absorption by biological tissues, NIR fluorescence is particularly useful in deep-tissue imaging, offering improved penetration depth and resolution. In medical applications, safety is one of the most important considerations. Naturally occurring and non-toxic BCDs are advantageous, especially when made from food ingredients, herbs, or plant waste. Several in vitro and in vivo investigations have shown that BCDs are preferable to heavy-metal-based quantum dots due to their low cytotoxicity, biocompatibility and biodegradability. However, for clinical translation, thorough toxicological screening is still required. Future studies must investigate the long-term storage of BCDs and their potential immunogenicity in animal models before moving on to human trials. It takes extensive biocompatibility testing to move BCDs from lab demonstrations to useful diagnostic tools. By considering these future objectives and views, researchers might overcome current constraints and broaden the applicability of naturally produced BCDs. The results will open the way for their broad usage in a variety of industrial, environmental, and healthcare fields, assisting in advancing science and technology while supporting sustainability and environmental conservation.

Bilipang Mahilary: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Khemnath Patir: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Sanjay Basumatary: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Methodology, Conceptualization.
